## Page 4

ii
PREFACE
Three censuses of population have been taken since the Government of Northern
Ireland was established.
The first enumeration took place in 1926 and incorporated
questions relating to occupation and industry, orphanhood and infirmities.
The seco|
enumeration made in 1937 was of more limited scope and was intended to bridge the gapl
between the census of 1926 and the census which it was proposed to take in 1941, but
which had to be abandoned owing to the outbreak of war.
The census taken as at mid-
night of 8th-9th April, 1951, forms the basis of this report .and like that in 1926
questions were asked as to the occupations and industries of the population.
The length of time required to process the data collected at an enumeration befo|
it can be presented in the ultimate reports is necessarily considerable.
In order tc
meet immediate requirements, however, two Preliminary Reports on the 1951 census were
published.
The first of these gave the population figures by administrative areas as
towns and villages, and by Counties and County Boroughs according to religious profes-
sion.
The Second Report, which was restricted to Counties and County Boroughs, gave
the population by age groups.
The present Volume is the second of seven county volumes (the Belfast County
Borough volume having been published a short time ago) and has been prepared (as were
also the Preliminary Reports) in accordance with the provisions of the Census Act
(Northern Ireland) 1951.
The statistics now published supersede those shown in the
Preliminary Reports and may be accepted as final.
This volume is consistent in design and scope with that published for the Belfast
County Borough and the remaining County volumes which will be published at intervals I
completed will be prepared in like manner.
1
A General Report for the whole of Northern Ireland will follow the county volume!
This will bring together, and in some respects show in greater detail, the informatiojj
contained in the county volumes.
Additional features will also be introduced parties
larly in regard to the distribution of the people industrially by age and marital
condition.
Following the 1926 census a topographical index was published dealing with each
type~bf area for which statistics were supplied in the county series.
A supplement
to this index was published after the 1937 census and a further supplement in respect
of the 1951 census will be prepared in due course.
Census publications are obtainable either directly from H.M, Stationery Office,
Chichester House, Chichester Street, Belfast, or from any book-seller.
L. C. MULLIGAN,
REGISTRAR-GENERAL
General Register Office,
Fermanagh House,
Belfast.
December, 1953.
(10S30)

## Page 7

PART I
EXPLANATORY NOTES AND DEFINITIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME
AREA
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey (foreshore being
excluded), and relate to the several administrative areas as constituted at the date
of the 1951 census.
The^ acreage covered by the larger rivers, lakes and tideways is
not included in the body of Tables 4 and 5 but is given in footnotes thereto.
POPULATION
The population c,6mprises all persons on land, in vessels on inland waters or in
port or at anchorage on census night, and also persons on coastwise voyages who arrived
in port within a brief period not having been enumerated elsewhere.
Members of the
Forces of the Croym are also included.
ADMINISTRATIVE DIVISIONS
The County is divided into sixteen county districts (three municipal boroughs,
six urban districts and seven rural districts) comprising 146 district electoral
divisions, twenty-three of which are coterminous with entire municipal boroughs or
urban districts or wards thereof (see table 4).
Wards, where they exist in boroughs
or urban districts are the units for the purpose of municipal elections.
In rural
districts the unit is the district electoral division.
The County is also divided
into twenty-three county electoral divisions, which are the units for the purpose of
county council elections (see table 5).
Owing to the abolition of the Boards of Guardians, details of the Poor Law Union
and Dispensary districts are not given in this volume.
These areas, however, form
respectively, superintendent registrars' and registrars' districts for the registration
of marriages, births and deaths, and consequently the census tabulations have been pre-
pared in a manner suitable for the extraction of data according to these areas should
such be required.
There are seven parliamentary constituencies for elections to the Northern Ireland
P&rliament, and two for elections to the United Kingdom Parliament (see table 9).
The Antrim Education Area is coterminous with the County.
The changes made in the constitution or boundaries of administrative divisions of
the County during the last intercensal period are as follows:-
(a) Ballymena Urban District was made a Municipal Borough (23rd May, 1939).
(b) Larne Urban District was made a Municipal Borough (23rd May, 1939); and the
boundary was extended (1st April, 1948).
(c) Carrickfergus Urban District was made a Municipal Borough (1st July, 1949),
the boundary having been extended (1st April, 1946).
(d) A portion of Larne Rural District was transferred to Carrickfergus Urban
District (1st April, 1946); and a portion to Larne Municipal Borough (1st April,
1948).
(e) The creation by the Representation of the People Act 1948 of two parliamentary
constituencies for the purpose of election to the Parliament of the United Kingdom.
0530)


---

## Page 8

HOUSES
1.
The term "House" means
(a) each structurally separate dwelling (except institutions) having distinct
access to the street, common stair, or landing.
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanitorium, nursing home, asylum, religious,
welfare or charitable community, residential school, barracks or other premises
under Naval, Military, Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other place of
detention), regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts.
(c) each boat, caravan, tent or other movable dwelling if occupied on census night
but not otherwise.
2.
The term 'Private House" means a dwelling other than an. institution, hotel,
boarding house, premises occupied by a caretaker, vessel, movable dwelling, barn,
stable or outhouse occupied on census night.
The term includes premises comprising a
shop with dwelling attached, unless occupied by a caretaker.
3.
The terms "Boarding House" and ^Tjodging House" cover habitations described as such,
and any private house containing three or more-boarders or lodgers.
4.
The term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons on census
night.
"Uninhabited" houses, therefore, include those temporarily vacant through
the absence of the usual occupiers as well as" those without tenants.
Enumerators were
instructed to omit from their enumeration empty houses in such an advanced state of
decay that there was little likelihood that they would again be made fit for^habitation.
ROOMS
1.
The rooms counted are the usual living rooms including bedrooms and kitchens but
excluding sculleries (i.e., compartments used only for washing, cleaning, or
cooking), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets, bathrooms, or any warehouse, office
or shop rooms.
2.
For the classification of households according to rooms occupied (see Table 7),
shared rooms - i.e., those used in common by two or more households - have been
included in the number of rooms occupied by the main household in the dwelling.
In
cases of one room occupations, all persons living therein, whether related or not,
have been enum.erated as one census household.
HOUSEHOLDS
1.
"Non-private Households" comprise all persons enumerated in hotels, boarding
houses, lodging houses, shops and institutions (see para. 1 (b) under "Houses"
definition above).
2.
"Private Households" are comprised of single persons or groups of persons occupying
separately the whole, or part of any private dwelling (see para. 2 under "Houses"
definition above).
Visitors and domestic servants are included in the household with
which they were enumerated.
Lodgers having or sharing separate accommodation were
enumerated on separate returns and consequently are regarded as separate private
households.
It should be specially noted that a household or private household for census
purposes includes any group of persons, whether related or not, who live together in
separate house or room accommodation.
RATEABLE VALUATION
The figures shown are those for the year ended 31st March, 1952, and have been
supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Subject to the provisions of the Local Government (Rating and Finance) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1929, agricultural land and agricultural buildings are completely
(10330)


---

## Page 9

derated and industrial hereditaments and freight-transport-hereditaments are partially
derated, the consequent deficiency in the yield of rates being made good by way of
General Exchequer contributions.
The figures in Tables 4 and 5 are the totals of the valuations in the various
areas before reduction on account of derating, but exclude in certain cases the valua-
tions of electricity, gas and water mains, telegraphs, telephones, fisheries, water-
works and tramways.
The total of such excluded valuations for the county is given
in a foot-note to the table.
USUAL RESIDENCE
The persons who have been regarded as having their usual residence in the area of
enumeration (i.e., the County) include:-
(l) Those enumerated in their own houses;
(2). Persons having settled residence in private lodgings;
(3) Resident domestic servants;
(4) Scholars., students, etc., living during term time in residential schools,
etc.;
(5) Inmates of institutions when the nature of the institution suggested
permanent residence;
(6) Crews of vessels if usually living on shipboard.
BIRTHPLACES
The birthplaces of a small number of persons were not stated on the census return;
it was assumed that these persons were born in the county or county borough of enumera-
tion, except when they were stated to be visitors to the household, or bore foreign
names.
After further enquiry in the latter cases it was found possible to classify
them under definite headings.
RELIGIONS
The classification of religions includes a separate heading for each religion, the
adherents of which number 10 or more (see Table 16). The heading "Presbyterian"
Includes those who call themselves by this name without qualification, together with
those who describe themselves as members of the Church of Scotland.
Other Presbyterian
denominations are shown at the foot of Table 16. Members of the Church of England and
ihe Episcopal Church of Scotland have been included under the heading "Church of Ireland".
the latter group includes all persons returned as ^'Church" or "Episcopalian.;"
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
There are two categories into T^Aiich the population may be grouped in their working
capacity, namely, occupation and industry.
The sole criterion for determining the
cccupational classification is the type of work performed.
On the other hand, industry
has to do with the kind of business with which the occupation is associated.
Thus a
carpenter working for a firm of shipbuilders would be classified under "Carpenter" for
occupation but under "Shipbuilding" for industry.
The 1951 Censuses were carried out simultaneously in all parts of the British Isles
and this afforded the first opportunity for Northern Ireland to adopt both occupational
and industrial classifications* on exactly the same lines as those used for the rest
of the United Kingdom.
* Classification of Occupations, 1950.
H.M. S.O.

## Page 10

In the preparation of Tables 19 and 21 the range of Orders, Sub-Orders and
OcQupational and Industrial unit groups have been modified by combining closely
related groups where the numbers were small and the preservation or usefulness of
the d^ta was not harmed.
TABLES CONTAINING DOTS (.) AND DASHES (-)
Throughout this volume dots (• ) are used in tabular matter to indicate "None",
and dashes (-) are used to indicate that figures are not available or that the column
headings are not relevant to the lines in which the dashes appear.
CORRECTION OF CENSUS DATA
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the census data.
The process of correction began with the enumera-i-
tors, who received full instructions as %o the action %o be taken in cases of error or
omission, and it was cpntinued by a system of check and further enquiry where necessarj
applied ^t each stage of the office work.
The process was completed by *an intensive
scrutiny and inter-comparison of the tabula-fion sheets.
This scrutiny and comparison
revealed a certain number of incpngruities, which, when material, were investigated an
corrected in the light of the facts disclosed; trifling errors were adjusted
arbitrar^y.
(10330)

## Page 11

PART II
STATISTICAL NOTES
The definitions etc., given in the preceding section apply equally to these notes
and to the tables in Part III.
APEA
Descriptions and figures are given for areas as constituted at the date of the
1951 Census, unless otherwise stated.
The area of Antrim County, exclusive of the larger rivers, lakes and tideways, is
1,098 square miles or 21 per cent of the area of Northern Ireland.
In 1896 the County
•nost almost nine square miles to Belfast County Borough, but gained at later dates an
aggregate of about half a square mile partly from County Down and partly from County
Londonderry (see note to Table 2).
POPULATION
The total population of the County was 231,149, an increase of slightly more than
J7 per cent since the preceding census in 1937 when the total population enumerated in the
Bounty was 197,266 (an increase of nearl}/ 3 per cent since 1926).
Table 2 shows that
censuses were taken decennially between 1821 and 1911, and thereafter at irregular
intervals and this should be borne in mind when comparing data of one enumeration
tith another.
Increases were shovm at the censuses held in 1831 and 1841 but through-
put the span 1841 to 1926 a decrease was registered for every intercensal period - the
Highest percentage decreases being 9.5 per cent for decade 1881 - 1891 and 8.9 per cent
From 1891 - 1901.
The lowest percentage decreases recorded occurred in the inter-

    tensal periods 1901 - 1911 and 1911 - 1926 the figure being I. 1 per cent, in each case.
In increase of 2.9 per cent was recorded at the 1937 census and 17.2 the largest

    fercentage increase (33,883 persons) ever recorded is in respect of period 1937 - 1951.
leference was made in the report for Belfast County Borough to the development of
Ihe Belfast area and it should be noted in connection with the increase in the popula-
lion of County Antrim that the population of the two Rural Districts, adjoining
lelfast, viz., Belfast and Lisburn, increased by 87 per cent and 35 per cent
iespectively (a total of 23,956 persons).
Natural Increase; The follov/ing table sets out figures of the natural increase
ind the intercensal variation in population in each of the intercensal periods since
1901.

## Page 12

Although data relative to the period 1821 - 1901 is not available for the County
as now defined, it is evident that in each of the intercensal periods from 1841 to

    1901, as well as from 1901 to 1926 there was a net outward movement of such dimensions
as not only to counterbalance the natural increase, but to cause an actual decrease u
population.
The position has, however, changed since 1926.
The net outward move-

    ment during the period 1926 - 1937 dropped to 7,110 from 18,50-2 in the preceding
intercensal period and a net inward movement of considerable dimensions occurred
during the period from 1937 to 1951.
It was proposed to include in'Table 3 of the county volumes the number of births
find deaths in each administrative area during the intercensal period, but as the com-
pletion of vital statistics was curtailed for reasons of economy during the war years,
it is not practicable to give this information without considerable delay.

f Estimated figure.
As the administrative d i s t r i c t s of the County are at present constituted,
165,672 persons l i v e in rural areas and 65,477 in urban areas.
The urban population

    constitutes 28.3 per cent of the whole as compared with 28.1 in 1937;
26.2 in 1926;
23.2 in 1911;
and 19.4 in 1901 - the areas being as constituted at the date of each
census.
Reference has, however, been made to the development of Belfast environs an
large t r a c t s of the two Rural D i s t r i c t s of County Antrim adjoining Belfast County
Borough are built-up areas.
I t is estimated that approximately 41,141 persons are
(10330)


---

## Page 13

iiving in these areas which increases the urban population of the county to 106,618
)r 46.1 per cent of the whole.
During the period 1901 - 1951, the population of each urban area in the County
^as increased, the largest increase (95 per cent) being recorded in Portrush Urban
district.
Each urban area also increased between the censuses of 1937 and 1951.
I
Four of the rural districts show decreases in population over the 1901 - 1951
leriod.
Two rural districts (Ballycastle and Ballymena) shov/ed fractional decreases
btween 193? and 1951, while the remaining five districts increased by percentages
^arying between 5 and 87.
It will be observed that the densities in the urban districts vary considerably,
the averages for Lisburn and Ballymena being 13.0 and 11.6 persons per acre respectively
iind that for Ballyclare only 2.3.
In the rural districts the rates vary between 0.1
pd 1.4 persons per acre.
The rates for the urban areas should, however, be used with
eserve in measuring the density of population, since the acreages of these may include
l^idely differing proportions of waste land, land under pasture etc.
Tables 4 and 5 provide detailed information regarding area, population, housing
bd accommodation for the various sub-divisions of the County and incorporate figures
n rateable valuation.
The rateable valuation of the County in 1951 was £1,411,319
efore reduction on account of derating and £910-, 242 after reduction on account of
prating.
In 1937 the corresponding valuations of the County were £1,097,994 and
[:624,085 respectively.
Table 4 includes information regarding Towns created under the Towns Improvement
Ireland) Act, 1854, and towns and villages containing 50- houses and upwards with no
egally defined boundaries.
The report on the 1937 census showed the population, etc.;, of the towns of
onglands or Coggrey, Whitehouse Upper and Whitewell which stand on the townlands of
lallygolan, Drumnadrough and Coll inward.
The development of these townlands for
fuilding since 1937 has been such that it is not now possible to segregate the various
owns standing on them.-
The following table, however, gives the aggregate figures for the
;owns of Longlands or Coggrey, Merville,"^ Whitehouse Upper, and Yftiitewell, but it
hould be borne in mind that the extent of the total area concerned was determined

Parliamentary Constituencies:
Table 9 sets out the Parliamentary Constituencies
f the County for the Parliaments of both the United Kingdom and Northern Ireland
[ogether with figures of population, number of electors and electors per 1,000 of the
lopulation, distinction being shown in respect of electors with residence qualification.
Under the provisions of the Elections and Franchise Act (Northern Ireland), 1946,
J person is entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any constitutency
'eturning a member to the parliament of Northern Ireland if he or she is a British
lubject, 21 or more years of age, not subject to any legal incapacity, and was born
jn Northern Ireland (or alternatively, has resided continuously in the United Kingdom
tor a period of seven years ending on the qualifying date) and either:-
(a)
has the requisite residence qualification of three months;
or
(b)
has occupied for three months prior to the qualifying date business premises
of at least £10 valuation;
or
•330)


---

## Page 14

(c) is the wife or husband of a person entitled to be registered in respect of
the above business premises qualification; or
(d) was on the qualifying date a member of the forces or a merchant seaman and
who, but for such service, would have been residing in Northern Ireland.
The electorate in the Queen^s University constituency comprises all graduates of
the University who are British subjects of full age, not subject to any legal
incapacity.
Under the provisions of the Representation of the People Act, (1949), a person
Ls entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any Northern Ireland
3onstituency returning a member to the Parliament at Westminster if that person is
a British subject or citizen of the Irish Republic of twenty-one or more years of age
and not subject to any legal incapacity to vote, is resident at an address in that
3onstituency on the qualifying date, and has resided at that address (or at any other
address or addresses in Northern Ireland) for a period of three months ending on the
:iualifying date.
In addition, a person who would ordinarily have been resident at a specified
address in a constituency in Northern Ireland, may be registered in respect of a
Service qualification if -
(i) he or she is a member of the Forces; or
(ii) he or she is a person employed in the service of the Crown in a post outside
the United Kingdom; or
(iii) she is the wife of a person having a Service qualification and is residing
outside the United Kingdom to be with her husband.
Education Areas; The Antrim County Council is the Education Authority for the
Arhole area under its control. • The arrangement whereby the county was divided into
five regional areas was abolished under the Education Act (Northern Ireland) 1947.
HOUSING - BUILDINGS, ROOMS, HOUSEHOLDS ETC.
Houses; The total number of houses, including hotels, institutions, etc.,
normally used for dwelling purposes was 61,879 of which 57,30-6 houses were occupied
Dn Census night.
The number of occupied houses at the 1937 Census was 46,769, an
increase therefore of 10,537, or 22.5 per cent under this category took place in the
intercensal period.
Table III includes all houses, private and non-private.
The
number of non-private houses was 563 of which 309 were boarding houses or hotels.
The population figures do not include inmates of institutions, but they do include
Dther occupants such as staff.

## Page 15

The intercensal increase in the number of inhabited houses of 22. 5-per cent
compares with an increase in population of 16.7 per cent and with an increase in census
households of 23. 0 per cent.
Generally speaking, therefore, a new house was provided
for each additional household accommodated in the County, but in considering the hous-
ing position the section of these notes vAiich deals with Inhabited Private Houses
should be studied.

    1,592 houses were in course of erection as compared with 225 in 1937.
    Of the 60v287 completed, houses, 2,981 were uninhabited on census night and as the
demand for houses in 1951 was so much greater than the supply general enquiries were
made as to why so many were unoccupied.
The following shows the position so far as
it can be ascertained:-
Houses from which the usual occupiers were absent
on census night, on holiday, etc.
Summer dwellings
Houses for sale or affected by change of occupancy
Houses about which no information was available
No enquiry was made as to the room accommodation of these houses.

during the intercensal period 1937 - 1951.

## Page 16

The average number of persons (exclusive of inmates of institutions) per house
has been steadily decreasing since 1851; it now stands at 3,98.
The size of the
average census household is also at its lowest point, viz., 3.95.
Table IV shows the changes in the number of houses in each urban and rural area
during the intercensal period 1937 - 1951.
As regards the increase in the number of
houses the remarks in the earlier part of these notes about the development of the
area surrounding Belfast should be borne in mind.
All districts show an increase in the number of houses.
In the urban areas the
greatest numerical increases took place in Carrickfergus (825), Lisburn (685) and
Ballymena (553) and the greatest relative increases in Carrickfergus (53.9 per cent),
Whitehead (53.5) and Portrush (22.0).
In the rural districts the greatest numerical
and relative increases took place in Belfast (4,021 additional houses representing an

    increase of 102.6 per cent) and Lisburn (1,889 and 33.9).
INHABITED PRIVATE HOUSES
Number of Houses and Inhabitants; The following Statement sets OUt the figures

The average increase in the number of inhabited private houses per year for the

    period 1926 - 1937 was 463 and for the period 1937 - 1951 it was 759 but this must be
viewed in the light of increases in population in these dwelling houses - the average
annual increase of which, for the first intercensal period was 643 whereas in the
latter it was 2, 263.
The figures for "persons per house", dropped during the period 1926 - 1937 by

    0. 35 and by 0-. <21 during 1937 - 1951.
    Of the total of 56,743 inhabited private houses 56,375 (99.4 per cent) were
occupied by single private households, the remainder 368 being shared by two or more
households.
This means that 98.6 per cent of the total private households
(i.e., 57,167) occupy a single house each.

## Page 17

The number of houses in each category increased during the last intercensal
period, though the increase in the houses with 1 - 3 rooms was relatively small
representing only 1 per cent compared with 38 per cent increase in the 4 - 5 room
group and 17 per cent in the remaining group.
The number of houses providing shared accommodation for households fell steeply

    between 1926 and 1937 and while the 1951 position is not so bad in this respect as
that in 1926 the number has almost trebled since 1937.
The number of shared houses
is, however, only 0.6 per cent of the total and it will be noted from the table above
that the majority are in the category containing 6 or more rooms.
Rooms: It will be observed from Table 6 that the total number of roomiS in

    inhabited private houses was 264,405 (the figure for 1937 was 212,709).
    The average number of rooms per house was 4. 66 compared with 4.61 in 1937.
The following table gives, for the County, the number of private households
occupying various numbers of rooms (irrespective of the number of houses) for the

    enumerations of 1926, 1937 and 1951:-

PRIVATE HOUSEHOLDS
Households: The population of 222,80-6 occupying private houses was comprised of
57,167 households within the definition of Private Households" given in Part I.
In
1937 there were 46,269 such households.
The increase of-10,898 households (23.6 per
cent) was at a raite greater than the increase in population comprising them (popula-
tion increase was 31,678 - 16.6 per cent).
The household factor rather than that of
population affects the question of housing.
In the period 1926 to 1937 the percen-
tage increase in households was 11.2 and that of the population comprising them 3.8.
The disparity, therefore, between growth in population and in households was much
smaller in the 1937 - 1951 intercensal period than in the earlier one.
Table 7 in which the measurement of households according to the number of persons
in each household is detailed shows that the size of household rises from 4,995 house-
holds of one person each to 11,633 households of the three person group.
The
households of four persons number 10,749 but from that point there is a sharp decline.
The three person group comprises 20.4 per cent of all households, the two person
group 19.6 per cent and the four person group 18.8 per cent - these three categories
make up 59.0- per cent of the total number of private households.
The comparable

    figures for 1937 wefe 19.0 for three person group, 18.4 for households of two and
16.7 for the four person group, making 54.1 in all.
The average size of household was 3.9 persons in 1951 and 4.1 in 1937.
(10B30)


---

## Page 18

XVI
DENSITIES
Table 7 also gives the composition of private households, the number of persons
comprising each household, the number of rooms occupied, and the density of occupation.
Private Households; The table shows that 31,0 per cent of the private households
occupied 4 rooms each, and that 22.7 per cent occupied one, two or three rooms each.
There were 110 households living at densities of more than four persons per room,

    478 households at more than three persons per room and 2,507 households or 4.89 per
cent fall within the over two persons per room category which has been assumed to
indicate overcrowding.
As shown earlier in these notes the household of three occurs most frequently;
but the most common combination consists of the household of four, each occupying four
rooms.
Of the 13,018 households in the 1 - 3 room group, 2,248 comprise only one
person each.
There are 483 households (921 persons) living in one-roomed accommodation, but of
these 269 are persons living alone, and 95 of the households comprise two persons each-

    in 1937 there were 507 households (900 persons) living in one-roomed accommodation.
The particulars of households in two and three-roomed accommodation are:-

Of the 56,743 Inhabited private houses 368 or 0.6 per cent were found to be
occupied by two or more households - 339 of these accommodated two households and
29 three or more households.
Persons; Of the total 222,806 persons in private houses 91.0 per cent

    (20-2,702 persons) were living at not more than two persons per room; in 1937 the
    percentage was 86.9, and in 1926 it was 81.6.
Table 7 also shows that there is an
average of 0.84 persons for each room which represents considerably more than one room
per person.

    In 1926 the corresponding figure was 0.99 and that for 1957 was 0.90.
Marked improvement in these respects has therefore been achieved during the period
1926 - 1951.

It will be noted that reductions - some substantial - in. the numbers of persons
per 1,000 living at a density of over 2 persons per room have occurred in each
district except Whitehead Urban since 1937.
All districts except Ballyclare Urban
and Ballymoney Urban showed a decrease between 1926 and 1937 but in both districts
considerable reductions have taken place in the subsequent period.
Despite the general improvement in the housing position it will be observed that
in the Urban District of Ballycastle and in the Rural Districts of Antrim, Ballycastle,
Ballymena and Ballymoney more than 100 persons per 1,000 are still living at a density
of over 2 persons per room.
The general situation is, however, a vast improvement on
conditions which existed in 1926.

* The 1937 figures relate to a Poor Law institution.
In the 1937 report attention was drawn to the drop of 13 per cent in the number
of hotels and boarding houses and of their occupants by 29 per cent.
Since that date
there has been a further drop of 37 per cent in the number of hotels and boarding
houses and of approximately 33 per cent in the number of occupants.
In Portrush the
number of hotels and boarding houses has dropped from 253 to 171 and of their occupants
from 1,116 to 773 but this town still contains more than half of the hotels and boardinj
houses in the County.
Ballycastle Urban and Rural districts show increases in the number of hotels, etc.
The figure has risen in respect of the former from, 13 to 22.
The increase since 1937 in both the number of inhabited buildings and population
under heading No. 12 ("Other Premises") will be noticed.
During the interval between
the censuses married quarters attached to War Department Establishments were constructei
as separate dwellings near to the main Services buildings.
Separate returns were com-
pleted in respect of these as they are individual houses but they are shown in Table 8
as they are attached to V/ar Department Establishments and, for the same reason, are
excluded from the Private House figures.
USUAL RESIDENCE
Table 10 is provided to show as far as the Northern Ireland enumeration can do so
the population normally resident in the County of Antrim.
Figures are given (for each
administrative area) in respect of persons enumerated in the County but usually resident
(10530)


---

## Page 21

in some other part of, or outside Northern Ireland, and of those usually resident in
the County but eniomerated elsewhere in Northern Ireland.
Although in 1951 Censuses
v^ere taken throughout the British Isles particulars of persons normally resident in
any part of Northern Ireland and enumerated in some other country are not yet available.
The table therefore is incomplete in this particular aspect.
From the table, however, it will be seen that of the total population of the

    County, 226,855 or 98.1 per cent were normally resident in the County and 2,961 or
1.3 per cent in some other part of Northern Ireland, leaving only 1,313 or 0.6 per
cent who-were usually resident outside the Province.
As the number of persons enumerated elsewhere in Northern Ireland but who usually
resided in County Antrim was 2,372 the adjustment to obtain the normal residence figure
was a net deduction of 609 on the total enumerated population or 2.6 P^r 1,000 persons.

    In 1937 the net deduction was 141 persons or 0.7 per 1,000 of the enumeration.
SEX, AGE AND MARITAL CONDITION
Table 11 shows the population of the. County according to sex, age (individual
years and quinquennial groups) and marital condition.
Table XI, although restricted
to certain age groups, provides a convenient basis for comparison of the 1937 position
with that of 1951.

## Page 22

Sex Proportions.
In this County females have been in the majority throughout
the whole series of enumerations, 1821 to 1951.
The ratios fluctuated from time to
time, the disparity being greatest in 1871 when there were 1,100 females to every
1,000 males.
The 1937 Report recorded the lowest figure which was 1,055 females to

    every 1,000 males; the 1951 is the next lowest figure, i.e., 1,060 females to
1,000 males.
Although the percentage increase since 1937 was greater for males than

    females in certain age-groups, particularly ages 35 to 54 and 75 and over (vide
Table XI) it is more than offset by the percentage increase of females in most other

    groups especially 20-24 and 55 to 74.
    Table 11 reveals that with the exception of the individual years 9, 15 and
17 boys outnumber girls up to the age of 19 years; thereafter at every year the

    females predominate numerically except at 31 and 37 years and also 96 at which point
the figures are the same for both sexes.
Under the age of one year there were 97 girls for every 100 boys.
At one year
the figure went down to 92 but at the age of 3 the difference was negligible, being

    almost 100 girls to 100 boys; at 4 years it was 95 and at 5 years of age the
differential increased again, to 93 girls to 100 boys.
In the quinquennial age-groups
under 20 years there was very little variation, the ratio remaining between 95 and
98 females to 100 males.
Looking at the higher age ranges there were 110 women to .

    every 100 men in the 45-49 age-group, 123 in the 60-64 age-group and 137 in the
85-89 group.
Ages.
Contrary to present day tendencies the average age of the population of
County Antrim decreased between 1937 and 1951.
In 1911 the average age was 30.0 years

    it went up to 30.6 years in 1926, to 33.4 in 1937 and fell to 32.6 in 1951, a decrease
of 0.8.
The following chart shows the d i s t r i b u t i o n of the population in the four main age-

    groups for 1901, 1937 and 1951.

---

## Page 23

It will be observed that in the two groups comprising persons aged 45 years and
upwards there has been a steady increase throughout although in the 65 years and over
group the increase between 1937 and 1951 was only 0.1 per cent whereas in Belfast
County Borough this group increased by 1.7 per cent.
The somewhat serious drop in

    the group 0-14 years between 1901 and 1937 has been checked and the 1937 - 1951 period
shows an increase of 0.7 per cent.
The 15-44 years range which increased slightly

    (0.1 per cent) in the 1901— 1937 period shows a decline of 1.3 per cent between 1937
and 1951.
The decline in Belfast County Borough for the same age group was 3.5 per

    cent for 1901 - 1937 and 3.8 for 1937 - 1951.
Table 11 gives the ages of the population by individual years and marital condi-
tion for the whole county and Table 12 gives quinquennial groupings according to age
and marital condition for administrative areas.
Of the quinquennial groups that from
birth to 4 years accounts for the highest percentage, i.e., 10.2.
In 1937 the
10-14 years age-group was the greatest and represented 9. 5 of the enumerated population.
The 1951 figures show an increase since 1937 in total population in every quinquennial
age-group except the 10-14 years group in which there was a decline from 9.3 per cent
to 7.7 per cent of the respective total enumerations.
In the groups of males,
however, decreases were registered not only in respect of 10-14 years but also for

    ages 60 to 69 and 95 to 99 but for females the only decrease occurred in the ages
10-19 groups.
For persons aged 100 years and over there was no change for either sex.
Marital Condition.
Since 1937 the number of persons in the County aged 14 years

    and over increased by 22,926, of which 10,60-6 were males and 12,320 were females.
The
number of single persons of the same age-group decreased by 2,521 (1,031 males and
1,490 females).
During the same period the number of married persons increased by

    24,139 (11,722 males and 12,417 females).
Single males aged 14 years and over accounted for 41.9 per cent of the male

    population of the same age in 1951 (49.6 in 1937) and married men for 53.6 per cent
(45.1 in 1937).
In the same category single females represented 39.2 per cent

    (47. 3 in 1937) and married women 50.5 per cent (42.5 in 1937).
For both sexes the only quinquennial group to show a decrease in married persons

    during the 1937 - 1951 intercensal period was the males group 60-64 years.
Widowers decreased by 130- whilst widows increased by 1,264.
The number of
persons whose marriages had been dissolved and who had not re-married increased from

    24 in 1937 to 198 in 1951 - divorced men numbered 57 and divorced women 141 in 1951
whereas the figure was 12 for each sex in 1937.
EDUCATION
The information provided by census returns regarding persons attending educational
establishments is summarised in Table 13.
The figures relative to numbers of persons
in full-time attendance are more or less in keeping with expectations, particularly in
respect of the ages 5 to 13 years, inclusive; within this group 4.1 per cent were not
attending school of which 3.9 per cent were aged 5 years.
The persons attending various educational establishments part-time were shown to
number 452.
As part-time students enrolled in the institutions of further education
under the management of the County Antrim Education Authority during the year ended

    31st July, 1951, numbered 5,014, it is apparent that in many cases the information
regarding part-time education was not furnished on the census returns.
Much the same result was disclosed by the report for Belfast County Borough but
it was then hoped that the position in the Counties would throw some light on the matter.
It would appear, however, that the significance of the question was not appreciated by
the heads of households.
BIRTHPLACES
Table 14 gives, by sex, the number of persons born in each of the Counties of
Northern Ireland, Commonwealth Countries, etc.. Foreign Countries, and at sea.
Of

    the total enumeration 69.9 per cent (161,514 persons) were born in the County; 21.9 per
cent (50,693 persons) were born in some other part of Northern Ireland and of these
(10330)


---

## Page 24

XXI1
30,334 or 13.1 per cent of the total population were born in Belfast County Borough.
Persons born in the Irish Republic numbered 6,583 or 2.8 per cent as compared with

    4,560 and 2.3 per cent in 1937, which represents an increase of 44 per cent in
14 years.
Only 0. 7 per cent were born outside the British Isles.
Persons born in Commonwealth Countries, etc. numbered 795, those born in foreign
countries 871 and those at sea 5.

From the above Table i t will be seen that the percentage of persons born in
Northern Ireland decreased whilst the percentages of those born in every other
country or group of countries increased.
Figures prior to 1901 are only available for all Ireland;
it will be seen,
however, that the 1951 percentage for persons born in Northern Ireland stands at i t s
lowest point for the half century and that for persons born in the Irish Republic has
reached its highest point.
It is not, however, prudent to attempt a final appraisal as to intercensal
fluctuations in a county abutting on a large city and the General Report will possibly
be more suitable for a statment of conclusions.

    / including Church of England and Episcopal Church of Scotland, numbering 2,813 and 11 respectively in 1951.
A scrutiny of Table XIV reveals that Roman Catholics were at their highest
percentage (24.3) of the total population in 1861; since that year the figures have
fluctuated, reaching their lowest point (20.1 per cent) in 1926.
The 1926 - 1937
(10330)


---

## Page 26

XXIV
interval showed an increase of only 0. 1 per cent but in the 1937 - 1951 period the

    increase was by 1.9 per cent to 22. 1 which is close to the 1881 figure of 22.2 per cent.
On the other hand the Presbyterian Church which also stood at its highest in 1861, viz.,

    53.4 per cent of the total eniameration, decreased to 44.9 per cent in 1951 which is the
lowest on record.
The Church of Ireland remained in 1951 the same as in 1937 viz.,
22.8 per cent which is its highest figure for the whole ninety years.
Methodists
attained to their highest percentage at 3.8 in 1951 having advanced somewhat steadily
from 1.5 in 1871.
The "Other and Not Stated Denominations" were 6.4 per cent of the
total population in 1951; this again is the peak for this category.
In brief, the
only groups that in 1951 exceeded their 1861 figures were the Church of Ireland,
Methodists and "Other and Not Stated Denominations".
Turning to the most recent intercensal period - 1937 to 1951 - the Presbyterian
Church was the only section to show a percentage decrease, although its percentage of
the total population is by far the greatest of all the groups.
The percentage
Presbyterians of the total population has, in fact, been steadily falling since 1861
but the most serious decrease (3.4) occurred in the last intercensal period.
Over
the last 50 years the population of the county increased by 35,059 but the number of
Presbyterians in the county increased by only 4,266.
As to the number of persons attached to each religious group, Roman Catholics

    increased by 11,177 since 1937, Presbyterians by 8,568, Church of Ireland by 7,758, "
Methodists by 2,805 and "Other and Not Stated Denominations" by 3,575.
Table 16 shows the population by sex for each Municipal Borough, Urban District
and Rural District under five religious descriptions, viz., Roman Catholic,
Presbyterian, Church of Ireland, Methodist, and all other (or not stated) denominations
The list of descriptions of "Other Denominations" given at the foot of the table
refers to the County as a vdiole; the names of denominations are restricted to those
having not less than ten adherents.
Viewing the figures numerically it will be seen that Presbyterians preponderate
in all municipal, urban and rural areas except Ballycastle Urban and Rural Districts
in which Roman Catholics are in the majority, and Lisburn Urban and Rural Districts in
which the Church of Ireland register the highest figures.
Of the "Other Denominations", the five categories which show the highest increases

    as compared with 1937 are: Congregationalists +• 904, Brethren + 581, Baptist + 453,
    Jews + 140 and Jehovah's Witness, who were not shown in 1937 but had 152 adherents in
1951.
The number of persons who omitted to state their denomination has increased
from 258 to 1,083.
Table 17 gives details for the County under five religious categories and age
groups (individual years to age 21, and quinquennial age groups up to 69 years).
The
comparative table in the 1937 report gave the population by individual years up to 20,
and by two religious groups - Roman Catholics and other denominations.
It is not
therefore possible to make a complete comparison between the 1937 figures and those
published in this report; the following list, however, shows the percentage increases
or decreases in the various age groups up to 19 years and of the population aged 20 and
over between 1937 and 1951:-

OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
As frequently mentioned in census reports the variety and complexity of occupations
and industries in which a population is engaged make it impossible to include in a
census report details of every kind of activity.
For Northern Ireland Reports the
volumes "Classification of Occupations" and "Classification of Industries" referred to
in Part I were adopted as these publications are in general use throughout the United
Kingdom and provide for uniformity of census data; their convenient arrangement of
headings - mainly according to materials worked with, commodities produced or services
rendered - is the most recent result of constant review and investigation.

## Page 27

The Northern Ireland census of 1926 was the last occasion on which occupational
and industrial enquiries were included in the census schedule and since then numerous
changes have been made in the classifications.
Special attention is drawn to the following points:-

    (a) The 1951 Tables deal with persons aged 14 years and over whereas in 1926 the
age of 12 years was adopted as the minimum.
For Table XV the 1926 figures were
adjusted to the 1951 age grouping but see the footnote to that table.
See also
footnote to Table XVI.
(b) A revision in the classification of occupations since 1926 brings together
the bulk of the unskilled workers in a single group - Order XXVI.
Occupations; The term "occupied" includes persons temporarily out of work.
In

    1926 there were 6,637 (4,829 males and 1,808 females) returned as temporarily out of
    work and in 1951 the figure was 4,851 (3,634 males and 1,217 females).
Table XV sets out the numbers and percentages of persons occupied (i.e., persons
whose normal occupation was stated) in certain age groups from 14 years and upwards for
the censuses 1926 and 1951.

13 vftio were occupied and wftio were also attending an Educational Establishment.
As the figures are not available for occupied persons according to age groups it
is not possible to supply for the counties the more interesting and detailed compari-
sons between the 1926 and 1951 enumerations.
Table 20 which refers to occupied persons
in age groups by marital condition is restricted to the Belfast County Borough volume
but the General Report will include a table for all Northern Ireland giving these
features.
The above table shows, however, that for both sexes the percentage of occupied to

    total number of persons aged 14 years and over is lower for 1951 than for 1926*
In
viewing this movement it is well to point out that whereas in 1926 males aged 65 years
and over comprised 11.8 per cent of the male population aged 14 years and upwards the

    1951 corresponding percentage was 12.7 and of females of 14 years and upwards the
    percentage aged 65 and over increased from 13.1 in 1926 to 14.1 in 1951.
Furthermore
it is probable that, as was the case in Belfast County Borough, the percentage of
occupied persons - both male and female - of the age group 65 and over decreased
considerably between the censuses.

* These figures Include occupied children aged 12 and 13 years - the' actual number Is not available, though based
on the number for the whole of Northern Ireland it Is necessarily small.
It is remarkable that the variations in percentages shown in Table XVI between 1926
and 1951 are so slight - the maximum variation being 0.3 per cent (a decrease in the
female occupied population).
There is only 0.1 per cent decrease in the whole occupied
population.
The figures for occupied persons as a percentage of the total population
(both sexes) of all ages in 1911 were - males 29.7;
females 11.0;
persons 40.7.
On
each figure there was an increase between 1911 and 1926 - an improvement which as stated
above, was maintained during the later intercensal period in respect of males only.

    In numbers there were 16,685 more persons at work in 1951 than in 1926 - 12,441
males and 4,244 females.
(10330)

In the absence of age group data by individual counties an analyti^ial study must
await the Northern Ireland figures as a vriiole.
Table 18 fiirnishes a complete list of occupations of persons 14 years of age and
over and Table XVII shows the proportions per 1,000 of the occupied males and females
under the numerically important orders and also for numerically important single or
grouped occupations.
As already stated the changes in classification have been so numerous since 1926
that it is not possible to give a comprehensive comparison with the data of that
census.
Nevertheless, in some instances the orders are, broadly speaking, on similar
lines and the following variations between 1926 and 1951 are significant:-
Occupation
Proportion oer 1,000 of
Occupied Males
Occupied Females
1926
1951
1926
1951
Agricultural Occupations
Transport
Personal Service
Makers of Foods, Drinks and Tobacco
* Note - The 1951 figures do not include unskilled workers engaged in the
manufacture of Foods and Drinks.
Orders II (Agriculture), VI (Metal Manufacture, Engineering, etc.) XVII (Transport),
XVIII (Commerce, Finance, etc.) XXVI (Unskilled occupations - not specified elsewhere)
provide employment for about 60 per cent of the occupied male population.
Agriculture
remains by far the most prominent of these Orders although the drop from 426 per 1,000

    of occupied males in 1926 to 261 in 1951 is disconcerting notwithstanding the fact that
Agricultural Machine Drivers, etc. increased from 14 at the former census to 158 at the
latter.
The figures in the female column of Table XVII reveal that Orders VII
(Textile Workers), IX (Makers of Textile Goods and Articles of Dress), XXII (Persons
engaged in Personal Service) and XXIII (Clerks, Typists, etc.) account for 66 per cent
of the occupied females; Orders XVIII (Commercial, Finance, etc. Occupations) and
XIX (Professional and Technical Occupations) account for a further 18 per cent.
Order VII (Textile Workers) heads the list, providing employment for 213 females out
of every 1,000 in occupations.
Turning to individual occupations the following variations since 1926 are
outstanding:-
Proportion per 1,000 of
occupied females
1926
1951
Weavers (including Carpet)
Nurses (other than Nursery)
Domestic Servants (indoor)
It is pointed out that in 1926 there were 119 male Agricultural Labourers per

    1,000 occupied males and in 1951 the figure was 117 for Agricultural Workers
(labourers, etc. not elsevrtiere specified), but in the former census farmers' sons,
daughters, etc. were separately coded - of these there were 111 males and 56 females
per 1,000 occupied respectively and in 1951 relatives were coded occupationally as if
there were no relationship - the 1951 figure for agricultural workers included farmers
relatives stated to be assisting in farm work of which no definite type was indicated.
Industries: Although the industrial classification was not subjected to such
widespread changes as took place with occupations the tables for both censuses were
not prepared on identical bases in that the former included persons temporarily out

    of work (4,829 males; 1,808 females) whereas the 1951 figures exclude persons out of
(10330)

work in accordance with the practice adopted for this census throughout the United
Kingdom; the data of the two census therefore are only comparable in a broad way.

groupings.

It will be seen that the Manufacturing Industries (the term is used in a wide
sense) is by far the greatest category of employment, providing work for 41 per cent
of occupied persons.
In 1926 the percentage was 35.
The 1951 percentage for

    Agriculture and Fishing - the next largest group - was 20 whereas in 1926 it was 33.
In actual numbers of persons there was a decrease from 27,417 in Agricultural and

    Fishing Industries in 1926 to 18,935 in 1951 and an increase from 28,909 in the
    Manufacturing Industries in 1926 to 38,774 in 1951.
The next largest group is that of Distributive Trades and Finance (including

    Insurance and Banking); in 1951 it accounted for 13 per cent and in 1926 it accounted
for 10 per cent of occupied persons.
As in the case of Belfast County Borough Table XVIII shows that the males out--
number females in all the groups except Professional and Miscellaneous Services.
The
former group includes Teachers and Nurses and the latter persons employed in Private
Domestic Service, Restaurants, Entertainment, etc.
A somewhat similar balance between the sexes obtained in 1926 although in the
Professions.the numbers of persons employed were almost equal, viz. 1,327 males and

    1,370 females - in 1951 there were 2,453 males and 3,294 females.
For males the Manufacturing Industries (mainly Textiles, Bui-lding and Contracting)
provided employment for 39 per cent of the occupied and Agriculture and Fishing 26 per

    cent., in 1926 the percentage for the former was 30 and for the latter 42.
The Manufacturing Industries (mainly Textiles and Clothing) employed 47 per cent
of the occupied females being the same percentage as in 1926.
Table 21 gives the industrial distribution of the occupied population in greater
detail, separate figures being given for each Order and suitable groupings thereunder.
(10330)

The figures given are for the County as constituted at the date of each Census,
exclusive of that part of Belfast within It.
Belfast, which was enlarged In

    1853 and 1896, became a County Borough In 1898.
The County boundaries were
extended by the transfer of
(a) the portion of Llsbum Urban District formerly situated In County Down -
pursuant to the Local Government (Ireland) Act, 1898.
(b) the portion of County Londonderry taken Into Portrush Urban District -
under the Local Government Board (Ireland) Provisional Order Confirmation
(No* 3) Act, 1914.
It will be noted that since 1911 the Censuses were taken at Irregular Intervals.

(b) The town of Ballygalley stands on the townland of Ballygalley, the figures for which Include those shown for the town.

## Page 123

PREFACE
Three censuses of population have been taken since the Governinent of Northern
Ireland was established.
The first enumeration took place in 1926 and incorporated
questions relating to occupation and industry, orphanhood and infirmities.
The second
enumeration made in 1937 was of m^ore limited scope and was intended to bridge the gap
between the census of 1926 and the census which it was proposed to take in 1941, but
which had to be abandoned owing to the outbreak of war.
The census taken as at mid-
night of 8th-9th April, 1951, forms the basis of this report and like that in 1926
questions were asked as to the occupations and industries of the population.
The length of time required to process the data collected at an enumeration before
it can be presented in the ultimate reports is necessarily considerable.
In order to
meet immediate requirements, however, two Preliminary Reports on the 1951 census were
published.
The first of these gave the population figures by administrative areas and
towns and villages, and by Counties and County Boroughs according to religious
profession.
The Second Report, which was restricted to Counties and County Boroughs,
gave the population by age groups.
The present volume is the fifth of seven county volumes (the Belfast County
Borough and Counties of Antrim, Down and Londonderry volumes having been published
already) and has been prepared (as were also the Preliminary Reports) in accordance
with the provisions of the Census Act (Northern Ireland) 1951.
The statistics now
published supersede those shown in the Preliminary Reports and m^y be accepted as
final.
This volume is consistent in design and scope with the previous volumes and the
remaining county volumes which will be published within the next few months will be
prepared in like manner.
A General Report for the whole of Northern Ireland xvill folloAr the county volumes.
This will bring together, and in some respects show in greater detail, the information
contained in the county volumes.
Additional features will also be introduced particu-
larly in regard to the distribution of the people industrially by age and marital
condition.
Following the 1926 census a topographical index was published dealing with each
type of area for which statistics were supplied in the county series.
A supplement to
this index was published after the 1937 census and a further supplement in respect of
the 1951 census will be prepared in due course.
Census publications are obtainable either directly from H.M. Stationery Office,
Chichester House, Chichester Street, Belfast, or from any book-seller.
L. C. iV!ULLIGAN,
•REGISTRAR-GENEPA.L
General Register Office,
Fermanagh House,
Belfast.
May, 1954,
(14699)

## Page 126

V I
PART I
EXPLANATORY NOTES AND DEFINITIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME
AREA
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey (foreshore being
excluded), and relate to the several administrative areas as constituted at the date
of the 1951 census.
The acreage covered by the larger rivers and lakes is not in-
cluded in the body of Tables 4 and 5 but is given in footnotes thereto.
POPULATION
The population comprises all persons on land, in vessels on inland waters or in
port or at anchorage on census night, and also persons on coastwise voyages who arrived
in port within a brief period not having been enumerated elsewhere.
Members of the
Forces of the Crown are also included.
ADMINISTRATIVE DIVISIONS
The County is divided into nine county districts (two municipal boroughs, three
urban districts and four rural districts) comprising 84 district electoral divisions,
nine of which are coterminous with entire municipal boroughs or urban districts or
wards thereof (see table 4).
Wards, where they exist in borotighs or urban districts,
are the units for the purpose of municipal elections.
In rural districts the unit is
the district electoral division.
The County is also divided into twenty county
electoral divisions, which are the units for the purpose of county council elections
(see table 5).
Owing to the abolition of the Boards of Guardians, details of the Poor Law Union
and Dispensary districts are not given in this volume.
These areas, however, form
respectively, superintendent registrars' and registrars' districts for the registration
of marriages, births and deaths, and consequently the census tabulations have been pre-
pared in a manner suitable for the extraction of data according to these areas should
such be required.
There are four parliamentary constituencies for elections to the Northern Ireland
Parliament, and one for elections to the United Kingdom Parliament (see table 9).
The Armagh Education Area is coterminous with the County.
The changes made in the constitution or boundaries of administrative divisions of
the County during the last intercensal period are as follows:-
(a) The boundary of Armagh Urban District was extended (1st April, 1945 and
nth June, 1946).
(b) The boundary of Keady Urban District was extended (1st April, 1948).
(c) A portion of Armagh Rural District was transferred to Armagh Urban District

    (1st April, 1945 and 11th June, 1946); and a portion to Keady Urban District
(1st April, 1948).
(d) Lurgan Urban District was made a Municipal Borough (7th July, 1949), the
boundary having been extended (1st April, 1938).
(e) Portadown Urban District was made a Municipal Borough (2nd October, 1947),
the boundary having been extended (1st April, 1947).
(f) A portion of Lurgan Rural District was transferred to Lurgan Urban District
(1st April, 1938); and a portion to Portadown Urban District (1st April, 1947).
(14699)


---

## Page 127

V l l
HOUSES
1.
The term "House" means
(a) each structurally separate dwelling (except institutions) having distinct
access to the street, common stair, or landing.
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanitorium, nursing home, asylum, religious,
welfare or charitable community, residental school, barracks or other premises
under Naval, Military, Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other place of
detention), regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts.
(c) each boat, caravan, tent or other movable dwelling if occupied on census
night but not otherwise.
2.
The term "Private House" means a dwelling other than an institution, hotel,
boarding house, premises occupied by a caretaker, vessel, movable dwelling, barn,
stable or outhouse occupied on census night.
The term includes premises comprising ^
shop with dwelling attached, unless occupied by a caretaker.
3.
The terms "Boarding House" and "Lodging House" cover habitations described as
such, and any private house containing three or more boarders or lodgers.
4.
The term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons on census
night.
"Uninhabited" houses, therefore, include those temporarily vacant through
the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
Eniunerators were
instructed to omit from their enumeration empty houses in such an advanced state of
decay that there was little likelihood that they would again be made fit for habitation.
ROOMS
1.
The rooms counted are the usual living rooms including bedrooms and kitchens but
excluding sculleries (i.e., compartments used only for washing, cleaning, or
cooking), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets, bathrooms, or any warehouse, office
or shop rocHTis.
2'. For the classification of households according to rooms occupied (see table 7),
shared rooms - i.e., those used in common by two or more households - have been
included in the number of rooms occupied by the main household in the dwelling.
In
cases of one room occupations, all persons living therein, whether related or not, have
been enumerated as one census household.
HOUSEHOLDS
1.
"Non-private Households" comprise all persons enumerated in hotels, boarding
houses, lodging houses, shops and institutions (see para. l(b) under "Houses"
definition above).
2.
"Private Households" are comprised of single persons or groups of persons occupy-
ing separately the whole, or part of any private dwelling (see para. 2 under
"Houses" definition above).
Visitors and domestic servants are included in the house-
hold with which they were enumerated.
Lodgers having or sharing separate accommodation
were enumerated on separate returns and consequently are regarded as separate private
households.
It should be specially noted that a household or private household for census pur-
poses includes any group of persons, whether related or not, who live together in
separate house or room accommodation.
RATEABLE VALUATION
The figures shown are those for the year ended 31st March, 1952, and have been
supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Subject to the provisions of the Local Government (Rating and Finance) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1929, agricultural land and agricultural buildings are completely
derated and industrial hereditaments and freight-transport hereditaments are partially
(14699)


---

## Page 128

V l l l
derated, the consequent deficiency in the yield of rates being made good by way of
General Exchequer contributions.
The figures in Tables 4 and 5 are the totals of the valuations in the various
areas before reduction on account of derating, but exclude in certain cases the valua-
tions of electricity, gas and water inains, telegraphs, telephones, fisheries, water-
works and tramways.
The total of such excluded valuations for the county is given in
a foot-note to the table.
USUAL RESIDENCE
The persons who have been regarded as having their usual residence in the area of
enumeration (i.e., the County) include:-
(1) Those enumerated in their own houses;
(2) Persons having settled residence in private lodgings;
(3) Resident domestic servants;
(4) Scholars, students, etc., living during term time in residential schools,
etc.;
(5) Inmates of institutions when the nature of the institution suggested permanent
residence;
(6) Crews of vessels if usually living on shipboard.
BIRTHPLACES
The birthplaces of a small number of persons were not stated on the census return;
it was assumed that these persons were born in the county or county borough of eniomera-*
tion, except when they were stated to be visitors to the household, or bore foreign
names.
After further enquiry in the latter cases it was found possible to classify
them (with one exception) under definite headings.
RELIGIONS
The classification of religions includes a separate heading for each religion, the
adherents of which number 10 or more (see Table 16).
The heading "Presbyterian" in-
cludes those who call themselves by this name without qualification, together with
those who describe themselves as members of the Church of Scotland.
Other Presbyterian
denominations are shown at the foot of Table 16.
Members of the Church of England and
the Episcopal Church of Scotland have been included under the heading "Church of
Ireland".
The latter group includes all pet^sons returned as "Church" or "Episcopalian".
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
There are two categories into which the population may be grouped in their working
capacity, namely, occupation and industry.
The sole-eritarion for determinir]g the
occupational classification is the type of work performed.
On the other hand, indus-
try has to do with the kind of business with which the oocupation is associated.
Thus
a carpenter working for a firm of shipbuilders would be classified under "Carpenter"
for occupation but under "Shipbuilding" for industry.
The 1951 Censuses were carried out simultaneously in all parts of the British Isles
and this afforded the first opportunity for Northern Ireland to adopt both occupational
and industrial classifications* on exactly the same lines as those ^sed for the rest of
the United Kingdom.
In the preparation of ikbles 19 and 21 the range of Orders, Sub^Orders and
Occupational and Industrial unit groups have been modified by combining closely related
groups where the numbers were small and the preservation or usefulness of the data was
not harmed.
* Classlflcatlcsi of Occupatlcms, 1950.
H.M.S.O.

## Page 129

TABLES CONTAINING DOTS (.) AND DASHES (-)
Throughout this volume dots (•) are used in tabular matter to indicate "None",
and dashes (-) are used to indicate that figures are not available or that the column
headings are not relevant to the lines in which the dashes appear.
CORRECTION OF CENSUS DATA
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the census data.
The process of correction began with the enumera-
tors, who received full instructions as to the action to be taken in cases of error or
omission, and it was continued by a system of check and further enquiry where necessary
applied at each stage of the office work.
The process was completed by an intensive
scrutiny and inter-comparison of the tabulation sheets.
This scrutiny and comparison
revealed a certain number of incongruities, which, when material, were investigated and
corrected in the light of the facts disclosed; trifling errors were adjusted
arbitrarily.
{14699)


---

## Page 130

PART II
STATISTICAL NOTES
The definitions, etc., given in the preceding section apply equally to these
notes and to the tables in Part III.
AREA
Descriptions and figures are given for areas as constituted at the date of the
1951 census unless otherwise stated.
It will be seen from Table 1 (Part III) that the total area of the County exclu-
sive of the larger rivers and lakes, is 489 square miles or 9.3 per cent of the area
of Northern Ireland.
In 1899 the County lost over half a square mile of Down County
(see note to table 2).
POPULATION
The total enumeration for the County was 114,254.
For each intercensal period
between 1841 and 1937 a decrease in population was recorded but the latest interval,

    i.e., 1937 - 1951 brought relief by showing an increase by 5,439 persons.
Table 2
shows that censuses were taken decennially between 1821 and 1911 and thereafter at
irregular intervals; this should be borne in mind when comparing data of one enumera-
tion with another.
During the whole range of enumerations the highest point was

    reached in 1841 when the figure was 233,024 or more than twice as high as in 1951.
    The two periods between 1821 and 1841 showed a combined increase of 35,597 persons but
    in the following period (1841-1851) there was a decrease of 36,709 and although this
was the greatest intercensal percentage decrease (15.8) the decreases for the periods

    1881 - 1891 and 1891 - 1901 were also high, being 12.2 and 12.5 respectively.
Natural Increase; The following table sets out figures of the natural increase
and actual increase or decrease of population in each of the intercensal periods since
1891, and shows the net loss by migration.

    1891, but it is evident that in each of the intercensal periods from 1841 to 1891, as

to 5,916 from 15,573 in the preceding intercensal period and although the latest period

* Tlie figures given relate to each area as constituted at the date of the 1951 Census.
f Estimated figure.
As the administrative divisions of the County are constituted at present, 68,733
live in rural areas and 45,521 in urban districts.
The urban population constitutes

    39.8 per cent of the whole as compared with 3'2.8 in 1937, 31.4 in 1926, 28.7 in 1911,
and 24.6 in 1901 - the areas being as consiituted at the date of each census.
It
will be seen that all divisions except Newry No.2 and Tanderagee Rural Districts show
increases during the fourteen years 1937 - 1951.
The most marked percentage
increases are in Tanderagee Urban District (24.5 per cent or 274 persons), Portadcwn

    Municipal Borough (16.2 per cent or 2,399 persons) and Keady Urban District (16.0 per
cent or 202 persons).
The total increase in the urban population is 5,297 or 13.2
per cent and the overall increase in the rural population is 142 or 0.2 per cent -
the areas being the same for both censuses.
Looking over the whole course of fifty years .(1901 - 1951) Armagh Urban District

    (455 or 5.2 per cent), Lurgan Municipal Borough (4,145 or 34.4 per cent), and
Portadown Municipal Borough (6,248 or 57.0 per cent) are the only urban areas to show
an increase since the commencement of the period.
All rural districts show substan-
tial decreases, notably T^inderagee (1,620 or 33.6 per cent).
Armagh had the lowest
proportionate decrease (7,665 persons or 21.0 per cent).
Amongst the urban areas population density is highest in Lurgan (10.9 persons per
acre) and lowest in Tanderagee (2.0 persons per acre).
In rural districts the rates
vary between 0.2 and 0.3 persons per acre.
For the whole County the rate is 0.4
persons per acre.
Tables 4 and 5 provide detailed information regarding area, population, housing
and accommodation for the various sub-divisions of the County and incorporate figures
of rateable valuation.
The rateable valuation of the Counrty in 1951 was £607,892
(14699)


---

## Page 132

before reduction on account of derating and £302,619 after reduction on account of
derating.
In 1937 the corresponding valuations of the County were £532,928 and
£234,937 respectively.
Table 4 includes information regarding towns and villages containing 50 houses
and upwards with no legally defined boundaries.
Parliamentary Constituencies: Table 9 sets out the Parliamentary Constituencies
of the County for the Parliaments of both the United Kingdom and Northern-Ireland to-
gether with the figures of population, number of electors and electors per 1,000 of
the population, distinction being shown in respect of electors with residence
qualification.
Under the provisions of the Elections and Franchise Act (Northern Ireland), 1946,
a person is entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any constituency
returning a member to the Parliament of Northern Ireland if he or she is a British sub-
ject, 21 or more years of age, not subject to any legal incapacity, and was born in
Northern Ireland (or alternatively, has resided continuously in the United Kingdom for
a period of seven years ending on the qualifying date) and either:-
(a) has the requisite residence qualification of three months; or
(b) has occupied for three months prior to the qualifying date business premises
of at least £10 valuation; or
(c) is the wife or husband of a person entitled to be registered in respect of
the above business premises qualification; or
(d) was on the qualifying date a member of the forceo or a merchant seaman and
who, but for such service, would have been residing in Northern Ireland.
The electorate in the Queen's University constituency comprises all graduates of
the University who are British subjects of full age, not subject to any legal
incapacity*
Under the provisions of the Representation of the People Act (1949), a person is
entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any Northern Ireland con-
stituency returning a member to the Parliament at Westminster if that person is a
British subject or citizen of the Irish Republic of twenty-one or more years of age
and not subject to any legal incapacity to vote, is resident at an address in that
constituency on the qualifying date, and has resided at that address (or at any other
address or addresses in Northern Ireland) for a period of three months ending on the
qualifying date.
In addition, a person who would ordinarily have been resident at a specified
address in a constituency in Northern Ireland, may be registered in respect of a
Service qualification if -
(i) he or she is a member of the Forces; or
(ii) he or she is a person employed in the service of the Crown in a post outside
the United Kingdom; or
(iii) she is the wife of a person having a Service qualification and is residing
outside the United Kingdom to be with her husband.
Education Areas: The Armagh County Council is the Education Authority for the
whole area under its control, and all figures given in this Volume for the County
apply equally to the Armagh Education Area.
HOUSING - BUILDINGS, ROOMS, HOUSEHOLDS, ETC.
Houses: The total number of houses, including hotels, institutions, etc., in
the County normally used for dwelling purposes was 50,359 of which 28,370 houses were
occupied on Census night.
The number of occupied houses at the 1937 Census was
26,850, an increase therefore of 1,520, or 5.7 per cent under this category took place
(14699)

in the intercensal period.
Table III includes all houses, private and non-private.
The number of non-private houses was 128 of which 38 were boarding houses or hotels.
The population figures do not include inmates of institutions, but they do include
other occupants such as staff.

That portion of
Newry Urban District situated In the County of Armagh was transferred to the County of Down In 1899.
The total number of houses was the highest recorded since 1901.
The number of
houses being built (742) was more than seven times as great as the next highest census
figure - that of 99 for the 1926 census.
Similar trends were observed in respect of
Counties covered by the earlier reports.
The average number of persons (exclusive of inmates of institutions) per house,

    steadily decreased from 1851 (when it stood at 5.53) and now stands at 3.98.
Table IV shows the changes in the numbers of houses in each County district
during the intercensal period 1937 - 1951.
The increase of 1,628 houses for the
County should be considered xvith the figure for uninhabited houses, which shows a

    drop from 1,825 in 1937 to 1,247 in 1951; 79 per cent of the houses unoccupied on
Census night in 1951 were in rural areas.

The average annual increase in the number of inhabited private houses for the

    period 1926 - 1937 was 130 and for the period 1937 - 1951 it was 115, but this must be
viewed in the light of variations in the population in these dwelling houses - there was
a slight decrease of population (g25 persons) in the first period, whereas in the
latter there was an increase of 5,872.
The relative changes are seen more clearly
from the figures for "persons per house" the ratios having dropped for the period

    1926 - 1937 by 0.25 and for 1937 - 1951 by 0.02.
Thus the rate of improvement in the
second period has been slower than in the first period.

    Of the total of 28,242 inhabited private houses, 28,044 (99.3 per cent) were
occupied by single private households, the remainder of 198 were shared by two or more
households.
This means that 98.5 per cent of the total private households (28,465)
each occupies a single house.

    Table V shows for the County the position at each census,1926, 1937 and 1951 for
inhabited private houses of various sizes and the extent to which they were shared by
two or more households.

1937 - 1951 periods.
The number of houses with four or five rooms has increased
steeply over the whole period, while the number of houses with six or more rooms has

1937 and 1951 it increased in each of the three categories.
