## Page 135

Information incorporated in Tables V and VI for 1951 can be readily obtained for
each district from Tables 6 and 7.
PRIVATE HOUSEHOLDS
Households: The population of 110,997 occupying private houses was comprised of
28,465 households within the definitions of "Private Households" given in Part I.
In
1937 there were 26,729 such households.
The increase of 1,736 households (6.5 per
cent) was at a somewhat greater rate than the rate of increase in population comprising
them (population increase was 5,872 -5.6 per cent).
The household factor rather than
that of population affects the question of housing.
In the period 1926 to 1937 the
percentage increase in households was 5.4, whereas the population comprising them showed
a slight decrease.
Households of all sizes increased in number during the period
1937 - 1951 with the exception of households of seven, nine and ten persons each.
Table 7, in which the measurement of households according to the number of persons
in each household is detailed, shows that the number of households rises from 3,233
households of one person each, to 5,632 households of the two person group; from thence
there is a decrease to 5,365 in the three person group from which the decline is more
marked as each group is reached.
The two person group comprises 19.8 per cent of all
households, the three person group 18.8 per cent and the four person group 16.5 per cent -
these three categories make up over 55 per cent of the total number of private haiseholds.

    The comparable figures for 1937 were 19.8 for the two person, 18.5 for three
person households and 16.1 for the four person group, making over 54 per cent in all.
The average size of household was 3.9 persons in 1951 and also in 1937.
DENSITIES
Table 7 gives the composition of private households, the number of persons com-
prising each household, the number of rooms occupied, and the density of occupation.
Private Households: There were 68 households living at densities of more than
four persons per room and 294 households at more than three persons per room.
1,617
households or 5. 7 per cent fall within the over two person per room category which
has been assumed to indicate overcrowding.
As shown earlier in these notes the household of two occurs most frequently; and
the most common combination consists of the household of two occupying four rooms.
Of the 8,656 households in the 1-3 room group, 1,727 comprise only one person each.
There are 265 households (509 persons) living in one roomed accommodation, but of these

    144 are persons living alone - the figures in 1937 were 302 households (541 persons)
living in one-roomed accommodation.
The number of households of two or more persons

    occupying one room increased from 124 in 1926 to 132 in 1937, but fell to 121. in 1951.
The particulars of households in two and three-roomed accommodation are:-

Of the 28,242 inhabited private houses 198 or 0.7 per cent were found to be
occupied by two or more households - 179 of these accommodated two households and 3 9
accommodated three or more households.

    Persons; Of the total of 110,997 persons in private houses 88.2 per cent (97,890
persons) were living not more than two persons per room: in 1937 the percentage was
86.5, so that a slight mitigation of general overcrowding is indicated.
Table 7 shows that, taking the total number of persons in private households and
the total number of rooms occupied, there is an average of 0.91 persons for each room.

    In 1926 the corresponding figure was 1.02 and that for 1937 was 0.93, so that there
has been an improvement in room accommodation during both intercensal periods, though
the rate of improvement was considerably higher in the first period.

County Districts; In the county divisions the proportion of households occupying
one room each is greatest in Newry No.2 Rural District where the percentage is 1.93

    (123 households comprised of 239 persons), but of these 68 are persons living alone.
In the remaining areas the proportions are all under one per cent.
Table IX shows, by districts, the population in private households living at two
persons and less per room and at over two persons per room, also persons per 1,000
living at these densities, and for comparison, the 1926 and 1937 figures for persons
per 1,000 living at a density of over two persons per room, are included.

It will be noted that in the County as a whole 118 persons per 1,000 live at a

    density of over two persons per room (in 1937 the figure was 135 and in 1926 it was
173 persons per 1,000 of the population).
Increases in the number of persons living
at this density occurred in Keady Urban District and Lurgan Municipal Borough in the
1937 - 1951 period.
Excluding Armagh Rural District, where no change is recorded,
decreases - some substantial - took place in all other districts.
Between 1926 and
1937 all areas recorded a decrease.
The greatest overcrowding is in the rural
districts of Newry No.2 and Lurgan and in Keady Urban District in which the persons

    per 1,000 are 169, 143 and 152 respectively.
The smallest proportions of overcrowd-
ing are in Tanderagee Urban District (51 persons per 1,000) and Tanderagee Rural
District (57 persons per 1,000).
(14<S99)

* The 1937 figures relate to Poor Law Institutions.
In the 1937 Report attention was drawn to the drop in .the number of hotels and
boarding houses, and their occupants since the 1926 Census.
Since 1937 there has
been a drop in every area and the total now stands at 38, as compared with 125 in
1937.
The drop in Armagh Urban District was from 33 to 14.
This area contains the
highest number of this class of building and accounts for 37 per cent of the total.

    Between 1937 and 1951 the number of inmates in institutions increased by 36, and
the total population (i.e., inmates and staff) of these institutions by 95.
USUAL RESIDENCE
Table 10 discloses that 98.4 per cent of the enumerated population of the County
were normally resident in the County and of the remainder, 1,180 were usually resident
in some other part of Northern Ireland.
Allowitig for 1,128 persons usually resident
in the County but enumerated elsewhere in Northern Ireland the adjustment to obtain
the usual residence figure represented 0.5 per 1,000 of the population.
In 1937,
when the "Usual Residence" question was first included in the Census Return, the
adjustment figure was 0.6 per 1,000 of the total enumeration.
Only 626 persons were usually resident outside the Province.
(14699)

Sex Proportions: Since 1821, when the first Census affecting Northern Ireland
was taken, the number of females in the County has exceeded the ninnber of males.
The

    greatest disparity was reached in 1881 when there were 1,101 females for every 1,000
males.
Each census from 1911 to 1937 (inclusive) has shown a lessening in the pre-
ponderance of females; in the latter year the figure stood at 1,005 per 1,000 males -

    the lowest on record - and in 1951 there was an increase to 1,026 females per 1,000
males.
Up to and including 19 years of age, boys were in the majority, in 1951,

    except at the ages of 1, 5, 11 and 18, at which girls were numerically superior and at
age of 3 where the figures were identical.
Under the age of one year for the County
there were 92 girls for every 100 boys, but the figures were almost identical at age

    of 4, when the boys numbered 1,181 and the girls 1,179.
At the age of 5 there were

    103 girls to 100 boys but at each year between 6 and 17, inclusive, other than age of
    11, there were less than 100 girls for every 100 boy&; the figures almost corres-
    ponded at age 14 when the boys numbered 1,003 and the girls 1,002.
    For the age group 25-34 there were 103 women for every 100 men and this increased
    to 111 to 100 men m the 55-64 group.
    At age 82 there were 125 women for 100 men.
(14699)


---

## Page 139

It will be noted from Table IS that in all the urban areas there is a preponder-
ance of females over males and in all the rural districts, except Lurgan, males out-
number females.
Ages: The average age of the population of the County decreased between 1937 and
1951.

    At the 1911 Census the average was 30.7 years; it rose to 31.0 in 1926 and to
    33.4 in 1937, falling to 32.5 in 1951.
    In 1937 the 10-14 age group accounted for the largest number of the population of
all quinquennial age groups containing 9.6 per cent of the enumerated population, as
compared with 8.7 in 1951.
In 1951 the age group from birth to four years exceeded
all others with 10.3 per cent of the population as compared with 9.0 in 1937.
Since

    1937 decreases are shown in the five age groups between 10 and 34 and also in the
    60-64, 65-69 and 95 and over groups.
Increases were recorded for males in the quin-

    quennial age groups comprising 0-9 years, 30-54, 70 to 94 and 100 years and over and
    for females in the age groups comprising 0-9 years, 25-29, 35-64 and 70-99 years.
The following chart shows the distribution of the population for the County in

    the four main age groups for 1901, 1937 a'nd 1951.

It will be seen that in the two groups comprising persons aged 45 years and up-
wards there has been a steady increase since 1901 - as was seen also in the Belfast
County Borough and Counties Antrim, Down and Londonderry Reports.
The increase, how-

    ever, in the 65 years and over group was only by 0.1 per cent between 1937 and 1951
    and in the 45-64 group by 0.5 per cent whereas the increase in the 0-14 years group
was 1.0 per cent in the same period and the drop in the 15-44 years group was 1.6
per cent.
(14699)


---

## Page 140

XX
Marital Conditiori: Since 1937 the number of persons in the County aged 14 and

    over increased by 2,687 of whom 699 were males and 1,988 females.
The number of

    single persons in this age group decreased by 2,920 (1,700 males and 1,220 females),
    while the number of married persons increased by 5,921 (2,752 males and 3,169 females).
Single males aged 14 and over accounted for 46.5 per cent of the male population

    of the same age in 1951 (51.5 per cent in 1937) and married men for 48.5 per cent
(42.5 in 1937).
In the same age group, single females represented 40.4 per cent

    (45.3 in 1937) and married women 48.4 per cent (43.0 in 1937).
For both sexes-the only quinquennial groups of married persons to show a decrease

    during the 1937 - 1951 intercensal period were males 15-19, 60--69 and aged 95-99 and
females 65-69.
Widowers decreased by 361 whilst widows increased by 3.
The number of persons
whose marriages had been dissolved and who had not remarried, increased from 9 in 1937

    to 53 in 1951 - divorced men numbered 15 as compared with 7 in 1937, and divorced
women 38 as against 2.
EDUCATION
Table 13 summarises the information furnished regarding attendance at educational
establishments.
It will be seen that for the County of children aged 5-13 inclusive
4.3 per cent are stated not to be attending school.
In the 14-17 age groups, i.e.,
amongst persons over the age for compulsory school attendance but of normal age for
attending grammar school, 69.8 per cent were not attending educational establishments.
Persons of all ages attending educational establishments part-time were shown to
number 150.
As the total number of part-time students enrolled in the institutions
of further education under the management of the County Armagh Education Authority

    during the year ended 31st July, L951, numbered 4,900, it is apparent that in many
cases the information regarding part-time education was not furnished on the census
returns.
Much the same result was disclosed by the earlier census reports.
It
would appear that the significance of the question was not appreciated by the heads of
households.
BIRTHPLACES
Table 14 shows, by sex, the number of persons born in each of the counties of
Northern Ireland, other parts of the British Isles, Commonwealth Countries, etc.,
Foreign Ccuntries and at sea.
Of the total population of the County (i.e., 114,254)

    persons born in the County numbered 90,477 (79.2 per cent), and altogether 104,923
(91.8 per cent) were born in some part of Northern Ireland.
5,165 persons (4.52 per

    cent) were born in the Irish Republic as compared with 4,739 (4.35 per cent) in 1937.
    Only 639 persons (0.56 per cent) were born outside the British Isles, of whom 250 were
born in Commonwealth Countries, 387 in Foreign Countries, 1 at sea and in one instance
the birthplace was not given.
^
The percentage representing persons born in the County (79.2) has decreased since
1937 (81.5); the percentage of persons born in adjoining Counties of Down and Tyrone
increased from 6. 9 to 7.5.
Table XII gives comparative figures for Censuses since 1861.
It will be noted that the percentage of the total population born in Northern

    Ireland decreased steadily between 1861 and 1926, viz., from 96.02 to 92.13.
Between

    1926 and 1937 there was an increase to 92.56 but this fell to 91.84 in 1951.
Over
the period 1901 to 1926 the percentage born in the Irish Republic steadily increased

    (3.93 to 4.83); between 1926 and 1937 there was a decrease to 4.35.
In the last in-
tercensal period there was an increase to 4.52.
The percentage born in England and

    Wales stands at 1.90 the highest figure reached during the ninety years 1861 - 1951;
that born in Scotland increased for each intercensal period during the span 1861 - 1926
but thereafter a decrease is recorded at each Census.
Table 15 shows the numbers of persons in each administrative area born outside
Northern Ireland, according to sex, duration of residence, whether residents in, or
visitors to Northern Ireland and divided into five religious groups.
(14699)

* The figures given relate to the County as constituted at the date of each Census.
For particulars of
territorial changes see note at head of Table 2.
f Including Church of England and Episcopal Church of Scotland, numbering 632 and 3 respectively In 1951.
A scrutiny of Table XIV reveals that the percentage of Roman Catholics has in-

    creased in each intercensal period since 1901 and that the 1951 figure of 46.4 per
cent was only exceeded in 3 861 and 1871.
Presbyterians now 15.0-, stood at 16.2 of
the total population in 1861 and at each succeeding Census showed slight variations
from this figure, until reaching its lowest percentage, i.e., 14.9 in 1937.
The

    Church of Ireland decreased 1.9 per cent between 1937 and 1951 (from 31.7 to 29.8) and
at the latter year stood at its lowest point.
Methodists increased by 0.2 per cent
of the total population between 1937 and 1951: the percentage has risen steadily since
1871 and the figure for 1951 (4.9) is the highest recorded.
"Other and Not Stated
Denominations" have increased at each enumeration and show a percentage of 3.9 which
is an increase of 0. 7 on the 1937 figure.
A comparison of the 1951 figures with
those for 1937 shows that there were numerical increases in four groups, viz., Roman

    Catholics 3,538, Presbyterians 945, Methodists 49J, others 894, while the Church of
Ireland group decreased by 429.
The total of 34,082 tov the Church of Ireland is
the lowest recorded.
Table 16 shows the population by sex for each County District under the five
religious groups, viz., Roman Catholic, Presbyterian, Church of Ireland (including
the Church of England and the Episcopal Church of Scotland), Methodist and all other
(or not stated) deno^iinations.
The list of descriptions o^ "Other Denominations"
given at the foot of the table refers to the County as a whole; the names of denomina-
tions are restricted to those having not less than ten -adherents.
Of the "Other Denominations" the two categories which show the highest increases

    as compared with 1937 are: Baptist + 299 and Brethren + 262.
The number of persons
who omitted to state their denomination has increased from 81 to 324.
Table 17 gives details for the County under five religious categories and age
groups (individual years to age 21, and quinquennial age groups up to 69 years).
The
comparative Table in the 1937 Report gave the population by individual ages ujy to 20,
and by two religious groups - Roman Catholic and other denominations.
It is not
therefore possible to make a complete comparison between the 1937 figures aiid those
published in this Report, but it is observed that the overall increase in the popula-

    tion of Roman Catholics up to the age of 20 is 2,303 or 11.7 per cent, while the in-
crease in the population of all other denominations up to that age is 198 or 0.9 per
cent.
The following are the percentage increases or decreases in the various age

    groups up to 19 years and of the population aged 20 and over between 1937 and 1951:-
(14699)

A comparison of Tables 15 and 16 shows that in the County 9.0 per cent of Roman
Catholics, 7.1 per cent of Presbyterians, 7.7 per cent of members of the Church of
Ireland, 5.8 per cent of Methodists, and 9.5 per cent of other and not stated denomina-
tions were born outside Northern Ireland.
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
As will be seen from the volumes "Classification of Occupations" and "Classifica-
tion of Industries" referred to in Part I of this Report the separate terms under which
workers are classified run into maiiy thousands.
This has occasioned modifications to
bring census data within manageable dimensions, and thus generally speaking the arrange-
ment of headings is according to class of materials worked with, commodities produced
or services rendered.
Special attention is drawn to the following changes since the 1926 Census which is
the last occasion on which occupational and industrial enquiries were included in the
Northern Ireland Census Schedule:-

    (1) The 1951 Tables deal with persons aged 14 years and over, whereas in 1926
the age of 12 was adopted as the minimiim.
For Table XV the 1926 figures were
adjusted to the 1951 age grouping to allow proper comparison but see footnote to
that table.
See footnote to Table XVI also.
(2) A revision in the Classification of Occupations since 1926 brings together
the bulk of the unskilled workers in a single group - Order XXVI.
Occupations: Table XV sets out the numbers and percentages of persons occupied
(i.e., persons whose normal occupation was stated) in the age group from 14 years and
upwards, for the Censuses 1926 and 1951.
The term "occupied" includes persons temporarily out of work.
The number re-

    turned as temporarily out of work in 1926 for the County was 4,516 (3,120 males and
1,396 females).

    In 1951, the figure amounted to 3,843 (2,913 males and 930 females).

* These are adjusted figures and may include a number (but If so, a very small number) of children aged 12
and 13 who are occupied and who were also attending an Educational Establishment.
The above table shows that for both sexes the percentage of occupied to total

    number of persons aged 14 years and over is lower for 1951 than for 1926.
In 1926,
however, males ages 65 years and over comprised 13.2 per cent of the male population

    aged 14 years and upwards, whereas in 1951 the percentage was 14.0 and of females ages
    14 years and upwards the percentage aged 65 and over increased from 14.0 in 1926 to
14.5 in 1951.
It is probable also that the percentage of occupied persons - both
male and female - of the age group 65 and over decreased considerably between the
Censuses.
Though the proportion of occupied males has decreased since 1926, the actual

    number occupied has increased from 34,570 in 1926 to 35,101 in 1951 - an increase of
(14699)


---

## Page 144

XXIV
531 persons.
A decrease of 1,240 is shown in the number of occupied females, making
an overall decrease of 709 in the number of occupied persons.

    In 1926 there were 47 occupied females to every 100 occupied males, while in
    1951 there were 43 occupied females to every 100 occupied males.
Table XVI shows the percentage of occupied to the total population, including
children under 14 years of age.

* These figures Include occupied clilldren aged 12 and 13 years - the actual number Is not avallahle though
based on the number for the whole of Northern Ireland It Is necessarily small.
From Table XVI it will be seen that the occupied represented 46.2 per cent of

    the population of both sexes and all ages in 1926 and in 1951 the percentage was 43.8.
In County Antrim the decrease was only by 0.1 per cent but in Belfast County Borough
it was 1.1 per cent, in County Down 1.6 and in County Londonderry 1.4 per cent.
The
figures for occupied persons as a percentage of the total population (both sexes) of

    all ages in 1911 were - males 31.1; females 13.2; persons 44.3.
The increases
recorded for both males and females between 1911 and 1926 were not maintained in the
later intercensal period and, in fact, lower percentages were employed in 1951 than
in 1911 as well as in 1926.
For males the percentage increase was 0.3 between 1911
and 1926 but for the subsequent period a decrease of 0.7 per cent was recorded and for
females there was an increase of 1.6 per cent in the former period but a drop of 1,7
in the latter.
Table 18 gives the full list of occupations of persons of 14 years of age and
over and Table XVII shows the proportions per 1,000 of the occupied males and females
under the numerically important Orders and also for numerically important single or
grouped occupations.
35.4 per cent of the occupied males are engaged in Agricultural Occupations;
8.2 per cent in Commercial, Finance and Insurance Occupations (excluding Clerical);
7.9 per cent are engaged as workers in Building and Contracting, etc., and 6.8 per
cent in Transport and Communications.
24.4 per cent of occupied females are employed
as Textile workers; 15.5 per cent on the manufacture of Textile Goods and Articles of
Dress; 14.8 per cent are in Personal Service; and 10.1 per cent are Clerks, Typists,
etc.
The changes since 1926 in the Classification of Occupations and especially the
introduction of Order XXVI bringing together groups of unskilled, workers not provided
for elsewhere (formerly included in various Orders) make it impracticable to give a
general comparison between 1926 and 1951 figures.
It is, however, possible to give
comparative figures in some instances.
The following are thos;e which show the
greatest variation in numbers and which are broadly similar to the 1926
classification:-

Industries; The industrial classification has not undergone widespread changes
since 1926 as was the case in the classification of occupations.
The 1926 and 1951
figures, however, are not strictly comparable as the 1926 figures for the occupied
population include persons out of work (i.e., 3,120 males and 1,396 females), whereas
the 1951 figures exclude persons cut of work in accordance with the practice throughout
the United Kingdom for this Census.
Table XVIII shows the distribution for 1951 according to certain main industrial
groupings for the County.

The Manufacturing Industries (used in a wide sense) provide employment for
42 per cent of the occupied population.
In 1926 the percentage was 35.
Agriculture
and Fishing provide 27 per cent and the Distributive Trades and Finance (including

    Insurance and Banking) 12 per cent; in 1926 the percentages were 39 and 9 respectively,
(14699)


---

## Page 147

Table XVIII also shows that the males outnumbered the females in all groups except
the Professions and Miscellaneous Services.
The former group includes Teachers and
Nurses and the latter, persons employed in Private Domestic Service, Restaurants,
Entertainment, etc.
The same balance was recorded in respect of.earlier Reports for
1951.
For males Agriculture and Fishing provided employment for 37 per cent of the
occupied, and the Manufacturing Industries (mainly Textiles, Building and Contracting

    and Manufacture of Foods) 35 per cent; in 1926 the percentage for the former was 49
land 26 for the latter.
The Manufacturing Industries (mainly Textiles and Clothing) gave employment to

    156 per cent of the occupied females; in 1926 the percentage was 55.
Table 21 gives the industrial distribution of the occupied population in greater
detail, separate figures being given for each Order and suitable groupings thereunder.
(14699)

(a)
This 'i'owniatia is divided between the D.E.D's. of BessbrooK North (54) and Bessbrook South (55).
(b)

    Bils town of Bessbrook. is In the D.E.D's. of Bessbrook North (54), Bessbrook South (55), Camlou^ (56), Derrymore (61) and Mullaghglass (75) In this Rural District.
The portion In Bessbrook North (54) D.E.D. stands on the Townland of Clogharevan;
the portion in Bessbrook South (55) D.E.D. stands on the Townlands of caogbarevan
and Ma^emahely;
and the portion In Camlough (56) D.E.D. stands on the Townland of Carrlckbrackan.
The figures for the various Townlands Include those shown for
the respective portions of the town.
The entire town contains 2,888 persons.

## Page 213

PREFACE
Three censuses of population have been taken since the Government of Northern
Ireland was established.
The first enumeration took place in 1926 and incorporated
questions relating to occupation and industry, orphanhood and infirmities.
The
second enumeration made in 1937 was of more limited scope and was intended to bridge
the gap between the census of 1926 and the census which it was proposed to take in
1941, but which had to be abandoned owing to the outbreak of war.
The census taken
as at midnight of 8th-9th April, 1951, forms the basis of this report and like that in
1926 questions were asked as to the occupations and industries of the population.
The length of time required to process the data collected at an enumeration
before it can be presented in the ultimate reports is necessarily considerable.
In
order to meet immediate requirements, however, two Preliminary Reports on the 1951
census were published.
The first of these gave the population figures by administra-
tive areas and towns and villages and by Counties and County Boroughs according to
religious profession.
The Second Report, which was restricted to Counties and County
Boroughs, gave the population by age groups.
The present Volume is the first of seven county volumes and has been prepared (as
were also the Preliminary Reports) in accordance with the provisions of the Census
Act (Northern Ireland), 1951.
The statistics now published supersede those shown in
the Preliminary Reports and may be accepted as final.
All the county volumes will be consistent with the present issue in design and
scope.
,
A General Report for the whole country will follow the county volumes.
This
will bring together, and in some respects show in greater detail, the information
contained in the county volumes.
Additional features will also be introduced,
particularly in regard to the distribution of the people industrially by age and
marital condition.
Following the 1926 census, a topographical index was published dealing with each
type of area for which statistics were supplied in the County series.
A supplement
to this index was published after the 1937 census, and a further supplement in
respect of the 1951 census will be prepared in due course.
Census publications are obtainable either directly from H.M. Stationery Office,
Chichester House, Chichester Street, Belfast, or from any book seller.
L. C. MULLIGAN,
Registrar-General.
General Register Office,
Fermanagh House,
BELFAST.
August, 1953.

## Page 216

PART I
EXPLANATORY NOTES AND DEFINITIONS OF
TERMS USED IN THIS VOLUME
AREA
The acreages given are as measured by the Ordnance Survey of 1937-38 (foreshore
being excluded), and relate to the several administrative areas as constituted at the
date of the 1951 Census.
The acreage covered by tidal water is not included in the
body of Table 4 but is given in the footnote thereto.
POPULATION
The population comprises all persons on land, in vessels on inland waters or in
port or at anchorage on census night, and also persons on coastwise voyages who
arrived in port within a brief period, not having been enumerated elsewhere.
Members of the Forces of the Crown are also included.
ADMINISTRATIVE DIVISIONS
The County Borough is divided into fifteen wards, and each ward is coterminous
with the district electoral division of the same name (see Table 4).
The wards form
units for the borough council elections.
Owing to the abolition of the Boards of Guardians, details of the Poor Law Union
and Dispensary Districts are not given in this volume.
These areas, however, form
respectively, superintendent registrars' and registrars' districts for the registra-
tion of marriages, births and deaths and consequently the census tabulations have been
prepared in a manner suitable for the extraction of data according to these areas,
should such be required.
The County Borough is divided into sixteen parliamentary constituencies for
elections to the Northern Ireland Parliament, and into four constituencies for
elections to the United Kingdom Parliament (see Table 9).
There are no county electoral divisions in the County Borough.
(County
electoral divisions are the areas into which counties are divided for the purpose of
county council elections).
The Belfast Education Area is coterminous with the County Borough.
No changes in the boundaries of administrative divisions of the County Borough
were made during the last iiltercensal period.
HOUSES
1.
The term "House" means
(a) each structurally separate dwelling (except institutions) having distinct
access to the street, common stair, or landing.
(b) each institution (viz., hospital, sanatorium, nursing home, asylum,
religious, welfare or charitable commiinity, residential school, barracks or other
premises under Naval, Military, Air Force, or Police discipline, prison or other
place of detention), regardless of the number of buildings within its precincts.
(c) each boat, caravan, tent, or other movable dwelling if occupied on census
night but not otherwise.
2,
The term "Private House" means a dwelling other than an institution, hotel,
boarding house, premises occupied by a caretaker, vessel, gnovable dwelling, barn,
stable or outhouse occupied on census night.
The term includes premises comprising a
shop with dwelling attached, unless occupied by a caretaker.
riQ n-^^Rfto /1


---

## Page 217

3.
The terms "Boarding House^^ and "Lodging House" cover habitations described as
such, and any private house containing three or more boarders or lodgers.
4.
The term "Inhabited" denotes actual residence of a person or persons on census
night.
"Uninhabited" houses, therefore, include those .temporarily vacant through
the absence of the usual occupiers as well as those without tenants.
Enumerators were
instructed to omit from their enumeration empty houses in such*an advanced state of
decay that there was little likelihood that they would again be made fit for habitation.
ROOMS
1.
The rooms counted are the usual living rooms including bedrooms and kitchens but
excluding sculleries (i.e., compartments used only for washing, cleaning, or cook-
ing), pantries, cloakrooms, landings, closets, bathrooms, or any warehouse, office or
shop rooms.
2.
For the classification of households according to rooms occupied (see Table 7),
shared rooms - i.e., those used in common by two or more households - have been
included in the number of rooms occupied by the main household in the dwelling.
In
cases of one room occupations, all persons living therein, whether related or not,
have been enimierated as one census household.
HOUSEHOLDS
1.
"Non-private Households" comprise all persons enumerated in hotels, boarding
houses, lodging houses, shops and institutions (see para. 1 (b) under "Houses"
definition above).
2.
"Private Households" are comprised of single persons or groups of persons occupy-
ing separately the whole, or part of any private dwelling (see para. 2 under
"Houses" definition above).
Visitors and domestic servants are included in the
household with which they were enumerated.
Lodgers having or sharing separate
accommodation were enumerated on separate returns and consequently are regarded as
separate private households.
It should be specially noted that a household or private household for census
purposes includes any group of persons, whether related or not, who live together in
separate house or room accommodation.
RATEABLE VALUATION
The figures shown are those for the year ended 31st March, 1952, and have been
supplied by the Commissioner of Valuation.
Subject to the provisions of the Local Government (Rating and Finance) Act
(Northern Ireland), 1929, agricultiaral land and agricultural buildings are completely
derated and industrial hereditaments and freight-transport hereditaments are partially
derated, the consequent deficiency in the yield of rates being made good by way of
General Exchequer contributions.
The figures in Table 4 are the totals of the valuations in the various areas
before reduction on account of derating, but exclude in certain cases the valuations
of electricity, gas and water mains, telegraphs, telephones, fisheries, waterworks and
tramways.
The total of such excluded valuations for the county is given in a foot-
note to the table.
USUAL RESIDENCE
The persons who have been regarded as having their usual residence in the area of
enumeration (i.e., the County Borough) include:-
(1) Those enumerated in their own houses;
(2) Persons having settled residence in private lodgings;
(3) Resident domestic servants;
(4) Scholars, students, etc., living during term time in residential schools,
etc.;
(5) Inmates of institutions when the nature of the institution suggested
permanent residence;
(6) Crews of vessels if usually living on shipboard.
DS 03560/1


---

## Page 218

BIRTHPLACES
The birthplaces of a small number of persons were not stated on the census return;
it was assumed that these persons were born in the county or county borough of enumera-
tion, except when they were stated to be visitors to the household, or bore foreign
names.
After further enquiry in the latter cases it was found possible to classify
them under definite headings,
RELIGIONS
The classification of religions includes a separate heading for each religion, the
adherents of which number 10 or more (see Table 16).
The heading "Presbyterian"
includes those who call themselves by this name without qualification, together with
those who describe themselves as members of the Church of Scotland.
Other Presbyterian
denominations are shown at the foot of Table 16.
Members of the Church of England and
the Episcopal Church of Scotland have been included uiider the heading "Church of
Ireland."
The latter group includes all persons returned as "Church" or
"Episcopalian. "
OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
There are two categories into which the population may be grouped in their working
capacity, namely, occupation and industry.
The sole criterion for determining the
occupational classification is the type of work performed.
On the other hand,
industry has to do with the kind of business with which the occupation is associated.
Thus a carpenter working for a firm of shipbuilders would be classified under
"Carpenter" for occupation but under "Shipbuilding" for industry.
The 1951 Censuses were carried out simultaneously in all parts of the British Isles
and this afforded the first opportunity for Northern Ireland to adopt both occupational
and industrial classifications* on exactly the same lines as those used for the rest of
the United Kingdom.

    In the preparation of Tables 19, 20 and 21 the range of Orders, Sub-Orders and
Occupational and Industrial unit groups have been modified by com^bining closely
related groups where the niombers were small and the preservation or usefulness of the
data was not harmed.
TABLES CONTAINING DOTS ( Q AND DASHES (-)
Throughout this volume dots (. ) are used in tabular matter to indicate "None",
and dashes (-) are used to indicate that figures are not available or that the column
headings are not relevant to the lines in which the dashes appear.
CORRECTION OF CENSUS DATA
Every effort has been made to eliminate, as far as practicable, errors and
inaccuracies from the census data.
The process of correction began with the
enumerators, who received full instructions as to the action to be taken in cases of
error or omissions, and it was continued by a system of, check and further enquiry
where necessary applied at each stage of the office work.
The process was completed
by an intensive scrutiny and inter-comparison of the tabulation sheets.
This scrutiny
and comparison revealed a certain number of incongruities, which, when material, were
investigated and corrected in the light of the facts disclosed; trifling errors were
adjusted arbitrarily.
*Classlflcatlon of Occupations, 1950.

## Page 219

PART II
STATISTICAL NOTES
The definitions, etc., given in the preceding section apply equally to these notes
and to the tables in Part III.
AREA
Descriptions and figures are given for areas as constituted at the date of the
1951 census, unless otherwise stated.
It will be seen from Table 1 (Part III) that of the total area of approximately
24 square miles (exclusive of tidal water) about 17^ square miles are occupied by
buildings, roads, etc. - the corresponding figure at the last census (1937) was
13^ square miles.
About 5 square miles are under crops and pastures as compared with
9 square miles in 1937.
POPULATION
The total population for the County Borough was^43,671.
This is the highest
figure recorded since 1821 when the first enumeration took place.
It will be seen from Table 2 that censuses were taken decennially between 1821
and 1911 and thereafter at irregular intervals; this should be borne in mind when
comparing data of one enumeration with another.
Throughout the whole span 1821 to
1951, an increase has been registered for every intercensal period - the highest

    percentages being 43.0 for decade 1821 to 1831 and 43.4 for 1861 to 1871.
Since 1901 the rate of increase has been steadily declining - that for the most

    recent interval 1937 to 1951 being only 1.3 per cent or 5,585 persons as compared with
    36.4 per cent or 22,935 between 1891 and 1901.
These latest figures, however, should
not be regarded as a true index to the growth of the city.
The County Borough
boundary has not altered since 1896 and this has resulted in the development of a
Greater Belfast (unrecognised by any statute) which merges into the several towns with
undefined boundaries adjoining the City in the Counties of Antrim and Down.
By add-
ing the increase since 1937 in the population of these surrounding districts to that
of the city proper a more realistic figure can be obtained of the City's expansion
during the intercensal period.
Table 4 of tDie Preliminary Report showed that on this
basis the increase amounted to 37,000 approximately^
Within its boundaries the City had in 1951 more than five times the population of
a century earlier when in 1851 the enumeration was 87,062.
The annual increase
between 1937 and 1951 was only 0.1. per cent whereas the annual average increase for
period 1926 to 1937 was 0.5 per cent.
Natural increasef: The following table sets out figures of the natural increase
and the actual increase of population in each of the intercensal periods since 1901,
and shows the net loss by migration.
Owing to the extension of the boundary in 1896,
it is not possible to show figures for periods prior to 1901 in respect of the area
now constituting the County Borough.

## Page 220

Although data relative to period 1821 to 1901 is not available for the city as
now defined it is clear that the natural increase was considerably augmented by a net
inward movement.
As will be seen above the intercensal increase for period 19G1-1911
is almost exactly balanced by the excess of births over deaths.
Each succeeding
intercensal period, however, shows a net outward movement of considerable dimensions;
the figures for the latest period (1937-1951) reveal the greatest incidence of outflow.

    In the 1937 Report it was observed that during the 1926-1957 interval the loss by .out-
ward migration consisted mainly of males; in the subsequent interval 1937-1951 the

    loss, i.e., 50,535 was fairly evenly borne, being 26,041 males and 24,494 females or
51.5 per cent and 48.5 per cent respectively.
It will also be observed that the decline in the average annual excess of births

    over deaths recorded in the 1926-1937 period has been arrested and the 1937-1951
figures show the highest average annual excess for any intercensal period within the
last 50 years.

Seven wards show increases of population and eight show decreases during the
fourteen years 1937-1951.
The most marked increases are in Clifton and Victoria Wards,

    the increase being 4,954 (or 10.6 per cent) in the former and 3,318 (or 9.8 per cent)
in the latter.
The greatest decrease is in Dock Ward which shows a figure of 2,771 or
15. 9 per cent.
Scanning the whole course of fifty years (1901 - 1951) eleven wards show an
increase in population since the commencement of the period, three of which increased
considerably, viz., Clifton (94.5 per cent) Ormeau {59.3 per cent) and Victoria
(75.2 per cent) and four wards, viz.. Court, Dock, St. George's and anithfield show
decreases. Dock the most affected decreasing by 31.4 per cent.
PS 03560/1


---

## Page 221

It will be observed that the ward densities vary over a wide range, the average
for Victoria being 14.6 persons per acre and that for Court 158.6.
The average for
the whole County Borough is 28.9 persons per acre which shows very little change from
the figure of 28.7 for 1937.
The four smallest wards, viz.. Court (107 acres). Dock (183 acres), St. George's
(134 acres) and Smithfield (93 acres) contain the highest densities, the figures being

    158.6, 80.3, 110.8 and 113.3 persons per acre, respectively.
On the other hand the
largest ward - Victoria (2,531 acres) - has only 14.6 persons per acre, which is the
lowest density of all the wards.
The densities of the remaining wards range between

    41.2 persons per acre in Falls (807 acres) and 20.0 persons per acre in Cromac
(l,141 acres).
By way of comparison with densities of cities in Grea^t Britain of size in popula-
tion approximately the same as that of Belfast, Edinburgh and Bristol provide suitable
examples.
In the case of Edinburgh the Report for the 1951 Census shows that with an
enumerated population of 466,761 a density of 14.4 persons per acre obtains.
The
ward densities range between 76.0 persons per acre in Central Leith and 3.1 in Colinton.
For Bristol (population 442,281) the Preliminary Report for England and Wales for the
1951 Census reveals that the density is 16.8 persons per acre.
Parliamentary Constituencies: Table 9 sets out the Parliamentary Constituencies
of Belfast for the Parliaments of both the United Kingdom and Northern Ireland
together with the figures of population, number of electors and electors per 1,000 of
the population, distinction being shown in respect of electors with residence
qualification.
Under the provisions of the Elections and Franchise Act (Northern Ireland), 1946,
a person is entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any constituency
returning a member to the Parliament of Northern Ireland if he or she is a British
subject, 21 or more years of age^ not subject to any legal incapacity, and was born in
Northern Ireland (or alternatively, has resided continuously in the United Kingdom for
a period of seven years ending on the qualifying date) and either:-
(a) has the requisite residence qualification of three months; or
(b) has occupied for three months prior to the qualifying date business premises
of at least £10 valuation; or
(c) is the wife or husband of a person entitled to be registered in respect of
the above business premises qualification; or
(d) was on the qualifying date a member of the forces or a merchant seaman and
who, but for such service, would have been residing in Northern Ireland.
The electorate in the Queen's University constituency comprises all graduates of
the University who are British subjects of full age, not subject to any legal incapa-
city.
Under the provisions of the Representation of the People Act, (1949), a person
is entitled to be registered as a parliamentary elector for any Northern Ireland con-
stituency returning a member to the Parliament at Westminster if that person is a
British subject or citizen of the Irish Republic of twenty-one or more years of age
and not subject to any legal incapacity to vote, is resident at an address in that
constituency on the qualifying date, and has resided at that address (or at any other
address in Northern Ireland) for a period of three months ending on the qualifying
date.
In addition,, a person who would ordinarily have been resident at a specified
address in a constituency in Northern Ireland, may be registered in respect of a
Service qualification if -
(i) he or she is a member of the Forces; or
(ii) he or she is a person employed in the service of the Crown in a post
outside the United Kingdom; or
(iii) she is the wife of a person having a Service qualification and is residing
outside the United Kingdom to be with her husband.


---

## Page 222

Education Area - The Belfast County Borough Council is th^ Education Authority
for the whole area under its control, and all figures given in this Volume for the
County Borough apply equally to the Belfast Education Area.
HOUSING - PUILDINGS, ROOMS, HOUSEHOLDS, ETC.
Houses - The total number of houses, including hotels, institutions, etc.,
normally used for dwelling purposes was 110,619 of which 108,080 houses were occupied
on Census night.
The number of occupied houses at the 1937 census was 101,876 - an
increase of 6,204 or 6.1 per cent.
Table III includes all houses, private and non-
private.
The number of non-private houses, however, was only 722 (of which 495 were
boarding houses and hotels) and as most institutions contain private dwellings
Table III and also Table IV may, in a general way, be interpreted as applicable to
housing of private census households; the population figures do not include inmates
of institutions but they do include other occupants such as staff.

*The figures given are for the Borough as constituted at the date of each census.
The boundary of the Borough was extended in 1853 and again in 1896.

    The increase of 4,084 houses during the 1937-1951 intercensal period is a net
figure and represents the excess of new houses over those demolished (by enemy action
or in the normal way) and those converted to purposes other than dwellings, for which
data is not available.
945 houses (all of which were "Private*Houses") were in the course of erection as
compared with 399 in 1937.
Of the 109,674 completed houses 1,594 (or L5per cent) were
uninhabited on census night and as the demand for houses in 1951 was so much greater
than the supply enquiries were made to find out why so many were unoccupied.
The
following is the r e s u l t : -

census night, in hospital, on holiday, etc.

1,594

## Page 223

The average number of persons (exclusive of inmates of institutions) per house has
been steadily decreasing since 1851; it liow stands at 4.07.
The 'size of the average
census household is also at its lowest point, viz., 3.88.
Table IV shows the changes in the numbers of houses in each ward during the inter-
censal period 1937 - 1951.
The increase of 4,084 houses for the whole County Borough should be considered

    with the figures for uninhabited houses which show a drop from 4,260 in 1937 to 1,594
in 1951.
The number of houses in course of erection in 1951 was more than twice as
many as at the former census.
As regards the increase in the number of houses, the
remarks in the earlier part of these Notes about the development of a Greater Belfast
should be borne in mind.

As was seen in Table II the greatest nximerical increase in population took place
in Clifton Ward, the figure being 4,954 but the increase in the number of houses was
only 546 in that ward: the explanation of the apparent discrepancy in the ratio lies
in the fact that in 1937 Clifton had the highest number of uninhabited houses - 1,027
whereas in 1951 there were only 194 sucb houses.
Court, Dock, St. Anne's,
St. George's and Smithfield Wards all show a decrease in the number of houses.
The highest percentage increase in the number of houses took place in Victoria

    where the figure is 13.2 or 1,115 additional houses since 1937.
Woodvale has the
next highest figure,.9.8 per cent (579 houses) followed by Duncairn, 7.6 per cent
(667 houses).
DS 03560/1

Number of Houses and Inhabitants; The following Statement Sets out the figures

The average annual increase in the number of inhabited-private houses for the

    period 1926 - 1937 was 1,844 and for the period 1937 - 1951 it was 489, but this must
be viewed in the light of increases in population in these dwelling houses - the
average annual increase of which for the first intercensal period was 2,765 whereas in
the latter it was only 674.
The relative changes are seen more clearly from the
figures for "persons per house," the ratios having dropped for the whole period 1926 -

    1937 by 0.67 and that for 1937 - 1951 by 0.18.
    Of the total of 107,358 inhabited private houses, 103,685 (96.6 per cent) were
occupied by single private households, the remainder 3,673 were shared by two or more
households.
This means that slightly over 92 per cent of the total private households
(i.e., 112,590) each occupied a single house, or to put in another way, there are
almost 105 households accommodated in every 100 inhabited private houses.

    Table V shows the position at each census, 1926, 1937 and 1951 for inhabited
private houses, for the County Borough, of various sizes and the extent to which they
were shared by two or more households.

As might have been expected the number of houses of five or less rooms has
increased in both intercensal periods.
Although the number of houses of six or more
rooms increased during the first period there was a decrease in the later period.
No
doubt many of the larger houses have been converted to purposes other than dwellings
and others have been adapted for combined shop with dwelling accommodation thereby
placing them in a group with fewer rooms than previously.
There was a considerable decrease in the amount of shared accommodation between
1926 and 1937, but it will be noted that, while the percentage of shared accommodation
in houses up to five rooms remained practically stationary, the figure for houses of
six rooms or more increased from 3.7 in 1937 to 8.0 in 1951.
Of the 2,121 houses
containing 9 or more rooms, almost 20 per cent are shared by two or more households
and nearly 7 per cent of the 18,634 houses with 6 - 8 rooms are sublet.
Rooms: From Table 6 it will be observed that the total number of rooms in

    inhabited private houses was 499,450 (the corresponding figure for 1937 was 474,167).
    The average number of rooms per house was 4.65 compared with 4.72 in 1937.
The following table gives, for the County Borough, the number of private house-
holds occupying various numbers of rooms (irrespective of the number of houses) for

    the enumerations of 1926, 1937 and 1951:-

PRIVATE HOUSEHOLDS
Households: The population of 428,255 occupying private houses was comprised of
112,590 households within the definition of "Private Households" given in Part I. In
1937 there were 104,157 such households.
The increase of 8,433 households (8*1 per
cent) was at a rate much greater than the rate of increase in population comprising
them, (population increase was 9,443 -2.3 per cent).
The household factor rather
than that of population affects the question of housing.
In the period 1926 to 1937
the percentage increase in households was 19.3 and that of population comprising them,
7.8.
The disparity, therefore, between growth in population and in households was
much greater in the 1937 - 1951 intercensal period than in the earlier one.
In con-
sidering this feature of increasing demand for household accommodation it should not be
overlooked, however, that the most outstanding increases in households since 1937 took
place in the one person per household group - the figure increasing from 6,383 to
9,530 - and within that group undoubtedly many were lodgers having their meals apart,
who rendered separate census returns.
Table 7, in vfcich the measurement of households according to the number of persons
in each household is detailed, shows that the size of household rises from 9,530 house-
holds of one person each to 23, 999 households of the three-person group.
The house-
holds of four persons number 22,063 but from that point there is a sharp decline. The
three-person group comprises 21.3 per cent of all households, the two person group
20.2 per cent and the four-person group comprises 19.6 per cent - these three categories
make up approximately 60 per cent of the total number of private households.
The average size of household was 3.8 persons in 1951 and 4.0 in 1937,
Table VII
shows the persons per household in each Ward in 1937 and 1951.
It will be noted that
the figures for two wards (Falls and S^ithfield) have increased and that for one ward
(Court) remains unaltered.
In the case of the remaining twelve wards there has been
a decrease in the average number of persons per household ranging from 0.1 to 0.4.

## Page 226

X V I
DENSITIES
Table 7 gives the composition of private households, the number of persons com-
prising each household, the number of room.s'occupied,, and the density of occupation.
Private Households - There VJere 332 households living at densities of more than
four persons per room and 1,088 households at more than three persons per room.
5,186
households or 4. 61 per cent fall within the over two persons per room category which
has been assumed to indicate overcrowding.
As shown earlier in these notes the household of three occurs most frequently;
and the mo'st common combination consists of the household of three, each occupying four
rooms.
Of the 20,075 households in the 1 - 3 room group 3,303 comprise only one per-
son each.
There are 2,677 households (6,417 persons) living in one-roomed accommoda-

    tion but of these 933 are persons living alone - the figures in 1937 were 1,346 house-
holds (2,862 persons) living in one-roomed accommodation.
The number of households of

    tv;o or more persons occupying one room fell from 2,037 to 760 between 1926 and 1937,
but rose to 1,744 in 1951.
The number of households living at a density of six or

    more persons in a single roomi fell from 120 in 1926 to 43 in 1937 and has now risen to
106.
The particulars of households in two and three-roomed accommodation are:-

Of the 107,358 inhabited private houses 3,673 or 3.4 per cent were found to be
occupied by two or more households - 2,789 of these accommodated two households and
884 accommodated three or more households.

    Persons: Of the total 428,255 persons in private houses 90.8 per cent (388,867
persons) were living not more than two persons per room; in 1937 the percentage was
91.5, thus a slight worsening is registered due no doubt, to the building difficulties
created by restrictions and through enemy action destruction.
Table 7 shows that there is an average of 0.86 persons for each room which is
considerably more than one room per person.
In 1926 the corresponding figure was

    1.01 and that for 1937 was 0.88.
The rate of improvement, in this general way, in
the period 1926 - 1937 was not, therefore, maintained during the later period of
1937 - 1951.

    Table VIII gives the densities of room occupation for 1926, 1937 and 1951
Censuses for the County Borough.

## Page 227

Taking the total number of persons in private households and the total number of
rooms occupied, the average number of persons per room decreased by 0.13 between 1926

    and 1937 and by only 0.02 between 1937 and 1951.
It will be seen from Table VIII that
each density category lost some ground between 1937 and 1951, although over the

    25 years 1926 to 1951 considerable improvement has been recorded.
Wards - Households occupying one room each are greatest in Court Ward being 8. 3
per cent of total number of households in the wards. The number of persons concerned
is 850 or 5. 3 per cent of the ward population.
Dock V/ard comes next, showing a percentage of 6.9 households with 584 persons or
4.2 per cent of the population.
Smithfield follows having 5.6 per cent of households occupying one room each with
a population of 369 or 3.6 per cent of the ward.
Table IX shows, by wards, the population in private households living at 2 persons
and less per room and over 2 persons per room, also persons per 1,000 living at these

Shankill, Victoria, Windsor and Woodvale are the only wards in which there was a
reduction in the number of persons per 1,000 living at density of over 2 persons per
room since 1937.
All wards showed a decrease between 1926 and 1937 and except in the
case of Falls all wards showed a decrease - some substantial - between 1926 and 1951.
As in 1926 and 1937 Smithfield has more overcrowding (i.e. of over 2 persons per
room) than any other ward.
As in the previous censuses Ormeau and Windsor continue to show the lowest figures
in the overcrowding category.

* The 1937 figures relate to Poor Law institutions
?) This figure Includes 18 inmates residing In the
institution's Hostel who were described In the Return as "Employees".
In the 1937 Report attention was drawn to the drop of 50 per cent in the number of
hotels and boarding houses and their occupants since the 1926 Census.
It was con-
sidered that this was due to the number of small houses built during the intercensal
period, these houses having absorbed a great many people hitherto living in boarding
houses as well as persons who previously occupied portions of houses.
During the 1937 - 1951 period a further decrease occurred in the number of hotels

    and boarding houses from 973 to 495 and the number of occupants from 8,062 to 6,233.
The explanation seems to lie in the fact that there was a marked increase in the number
of households sharing houses in respect of which separate returns were furnished for
each household.
The following statement shows comparative figures for 1937 and 1951.

## Page 229

The increase in households was 2,559 whereas the increase in the number of shared
houses was only 976; the number of houses shared by three or more households increased
from 521 to 884.
USUAL RESIDENCE
Table 10 discloses that 98.7 per cent of the enumerated population of Belfast were
normally resident in the County Borough and of the remainder only 3,475 persons (0.8 per
cent of the total population) usually resided outside Northern Ireland.
The niomber of persons entimerated elsewhere in Northern Ireland but normally resi-
dent in Belfast was 3,445 and the number usually resident elsewhere in Northern Ireland
but enumerated in Belfast was 2,207; the adjustment to obtain the usual residence
figure represented 2.8 per 1,000 of the population.
The corresponding figures for
1937, when the "Usual Residence" question was first included in the Census Return, were

    2,191 and 2,032 respectively - the adjustment representing 0.4 per 1,000 of the total
enumeration.
SEX, AGE AND MARITAL CONDITION
Table 11 shows the population of the County Borough according to sex, age (indi-
vidual years and quinquennial groups) and marital condition.

restricted to certain age groups, provides a convenient basis for comparison of the
1937 position with that of 1951.

## Page 230

Sex Proportions.
Since 1821, When the first census affecting Northern Ireland
was taken, the number of females has exceeded the number of males.
The greatest dis-

    parity was reached in 1881 when there were 1,194 females to every 1,000 males.
Since
that year the preponderance has been substantially reduced - the latest figure, that

    for 1951, is the lowest recorded, viz., 1,117 females for every 1,000 males.
Up to
and including 17 years of age, boys are in the majority.
Under the age of 1 year

    there were 94 girls for every 100 boys but the figure increased to 96 to every 100 boys
    between 5 and 9 years and to 97 between the ages of 10 and 14.
From 18 years females
outnumbered males of all ages.

    In the 65-69 age group there were 138 women to every
    100 men, rising to 169 in the 80 to 84 years group and attaining £30 per 100 men in the
85 to 89 group.
Ages.
As experienced elsewhere in the United Kingdom the tendency in Belfast
County Borough is for'the average age to increase.
At the 1911 census the average was

    26.7 years; it. rose to 28.5 in 1926; to 31.7 in 1937; and to 32.5 in 1951.
In 1937
the 10 to 14 age group accounted for the largest number of -^he population of all quin-
quennial age groups, containing 9.6 per cent of the enumerated population, as compared
with 8.0 in 1951.
In 1951 the age group from birth to 4 years exceeded all others with 9.2 per cent
of the population as compared with 8.7 in 1937.
Since 1937 the two five-yearly groups
comprising children under 10 years are marked by increases, but all other quinquennial
groups under 40 years decreased during the intercensal period, thereafter there was an
increase in all age groups except 100 years and over.
Increases were also recorded

    for both sexes of 40 and over save in the case of males aged 60 to 69, the figures for '
which decreased slightly.
The following chart shows the distribution of the population

    for the County Borough in the four main age groups for 1901, 1937 and 1951.

## Page 231

Marital Condition.
Since 1937 the number of persons in Belfast aged 14 years

    and over increased by 6,997, of which 4,508 were males and 2,489 were females.
The

    number of single persons of the same age group decreased by 15,982 ^5, 293 males
and 10,689 females). During the same period the number of married persons increased

    by 20,981 - males numbering 10,227 and females 10,754.
Single males aged 14 years and over accounted for 37.7 per cent of the male

    population of the same age in 1951, (42.4 in 1937) and married men for 57.4 per cent
(52.2 in 1937).
In the same age category single females represented 37.3 per cent

    (43.8 in 1937) and married women 50.7 per cent (45.4 in 1937).
Widowers decreased by 644 whilst widows increased by 2,048.
The number of
persons whose marriages had been dissolved, and who had not re-married increased from

    68 in 1937 to 662 in 1951 - divorced men numbered 248 as compared with 30 in 1937 and
divorced women 414 as against 38.
EDUCATION
Table 13 summarises the information furnished regarding attendance at educational
establishments.
It will be seen that of the children aged 5 to 13 inclusive 3.7 per
cent are not stated to be attending school.
The persons attending various kinds of educational establishments part-time were
shown to number 1,275.
As the total number of part-time students enrolled in the
institutions of further education under the management of the Belfast Education

    Authority during the year ended 31st July, 1951, was 9,459, it is apparent that in
many cases the information regarding part-time education was not furnished on the
census returns.
It may prove that when the picture for the whole Province is complete some point
will emerge which will enable a general conclusion to be drawn as to the failure to
supply information on this head, and, if so, the matter will be dealt with in the
General Report on the Census.
BIRTHPLACES
Table 14 shows, by sex, the number of persons born in each of the counties of
Northern Ireland, other parts of the British Isles, Commonwealth Countries, etc.,
foreign countries and at sea.
Of the total population of Belfast, (i.e., 443,671),
persons born in the County Borough numbered 327,459 (73.8 per cent),, and altogether
400,135 (90. 2 per cent) were born, in some part of Northern.Ireland.
44,967 persons
(10.2 per cent) were born in the adjacent counties of Antrim and Down, 27,709 (6.2 per
cent) were born in th@ other four counties of Northern Ireland, and 17,590 persons
(4.0 per cent) were born in the Irish Republic.
Only 0. 8 per cent were born outside
the British Isles.
Persons born in Commonwealth Countries, etc. numbered 1,617,
those born in foreign countries 1,965, and 10 were born at sea.
No birth place was
given for 3 persons.
The percentage representing the persons born in the County Borough increased
since 1937, whilst the percentage for Antrim and Down and indeed all other counties
of Northern Ireland and the Irish Republic decreased.
Table XII gives comparative figures for censuses since 1861.

The Borough
It will be seen that since 1861 there have not been any marked variations in the
proportions of the population born in the several parts of the British Isles and abroad.
The tendency, referred to in the 1937 Report, for the proportion born in the Irish
Republic to fall, continues, and in fact the 1951 figure (3.96 per cent) stands at its
lowest for the whole period shown in the Table.
The percentage born in Northern Ireland

    is the highest since 1861 i.e., 90.19.
Table 15 shows the numbers of persons in each ward born outside Northern Ireland,
according to sex, duration of residence, whether residents in, or visitors to
Northern Ireland, and divided into five religious groups.
Table XIII gives for the County Borough, for purpose of comparison, the total
number of persons born outside Northern Ireland by duration of residence for censuses
1937 and 1951.

•/Including Church of England and Episcopal Church of Scotland, numbering

    4,760 and 6, respectively, in 1951.
It will be observed that in respect of both Presbyterians and Church of Ireland the
proportions of the total population have fallen since 1937, where^as the remaining three
groups have increased.
Roman Catholics have increased by 10,657 while the net decrease
for the combined remaining four groups is 5,072.
Of the "Other Denominations," the two categories which show the highest increases

    as compared with 1937 are: Baptist + 894 and Jehovah's Witness + 181.
The number of
persons who omitted to state their denomination has increased from 1,216 to 2,457.
Table 17 gives details for the County Borough under five religious categories and
age groups (individual years to age 21, and quinquennial age groups up to 69 years).
The comparative Table in the 1937 Report gave the population by individual ages up to
20, and by two religious groups - Roman Catholics and other denominations.
It is not
therefore possible to make a complete comparison between the 1937 figures and those
published in this Report, but it has been observed that the overall increase in the
population of Roman Catholics up to the age of 19 is 8.6 per cent, while the decrease in
the population of all other denominations up to that age is 9.1 per cent.

## Page 234

OCCUPATIONS AND INDUSTRIES
As will be seen from the volumes "Classification of Occupations" and "Classifica-
tion of Industries" referred to in Part I of•this Report the separate terms under which
workers are classified run into many thousands.
This has occasioned modifications to
bring census data within manageable dimensions, and thus generally speaking the arrange-
ment of headings is according to class of materials worked with, commodities produced
or services rendered.
Special attention is draiAm to the following changes since the 1926 Census which is
the last occasion on which occupational and industrial enquiries were included in the
Northern Ireland Census Schedule:-

    (1) The 1951 Tables deal with persons aged 14 years and over, whereas in 1926 the
age of 12 was adopted as the minimum.
For Table XV the 1926 figures were adjusted
to the 1951 age grouping -to allow proper comparison.
See footnote to Table XVI.
(2) A revision in the Classification of Occupations since 1926 brings together
the bulk of the unskilled workers in a single group - Order XXVI.
Occupations: Table XV sets out the numbers and percentages of persons occupied
(i.e., persons whose normal occupation was stated) in certain age groups from 14 years
and upwards for the Censuses 1926 and 1951.
The term "occupied" includes persons temporarily out of work.
The number returned

    as temporarily out of work in 1926 was 36,545 (26,254 males and 10,291 females).
In

    1951, the figure amounted to 13,904 (9,723 males and 4,181 females).

There has been a gradual decline in.the total number of both sexes in age groups

    14 - 19 and 20 - 24 since 1926.
Between 1926 and 1951 the percentage occupied in the

    male group aged 14 - 19 increased from 73.5 to 79.3 and in the female group from 71.7
to 81.8.

    It is of particular interest to note that in 1926 boys aged 14 and 15 stated
to be following an occupation formed 45 per cent of the total number of boys of those
ages while in 1951 the figure was 62 per cent.
The figures for girls of the same ages
were 49 and 68 respectively.
The explanation of the substantial increase in the
numbers employed may lie in the fact that the number of persons returned as out of work
in 1926 was almost three times greater than the corresponding figure for 1951 and thus
many boys and girls who had left school on attaining 14 years of age had not found
employment when the 1926 Census was taken and were returned as unoccupied.
The percentage of males and females between 20 and 24 years of age returned as
occupied decreased slightly, as did the figure for females aged between 25 and 44, but
the percentage for males between those ages returned as occupied increased by 0. 7 per
cent.
DP 03560/1


---

## Page 235

X X V

    The total number of males in the age group 25-64 increased by 12,037 (13,6 per
    cent) between 1926 and 1951 and the number of females by 13,917 (13,4 per cent).
The
total number of occupied men in that group rose by 12,214 and occupied women by 3,009.
The percentage of occupied men to the total rose slightly, but the number of occupied
women to the total fell from 38.9 to 36.8.
It should, however, be noted that the
number of married women in the age group rose from 60.6 to 66.8 per cent between the
two censuses.
As regards actual numbers more men of 65 years and over were employed in 1951 than
in 1926, but as the total number of males in that age group had increased by 83 per
cent between the two censuses, numbers alone are not a true guide.
The percentage

    occupied fell from 60 in 1926 to 35 in 1951.
In the case of women both numbers and
percentage occupied fell considerably between 1926 and 1951, the percentage figure to
slightly less than half.
These decreases are doubtless due to the introduction of
contributory pensions schemes.
For the whole range of ages, there was a decrease in the percentage of occupied
persons.
In the case of males the figure fell by 2.3 and by 4. 6 for females.
Table XVI shows the percentages of occupied to the total population including
children under 14 years of age.

## Page 237

Approximately 46 per cent of the occupied males are to be found under three Orders:
VI, Workers in Metal, Engineering and Allied' Trades; XVII, Persons engaged in Trans-
port and Communication; XXVI, Workers in unskilled occupations (not specified under
other Orders).
About 70 per cent of the occupied females are included in four Orders:
VII, Textile Workers; IX, Makers of Textile Goods and Articles of Dress; XXII Per-
sonal Service; XXIII Clerks, Typists etc.
The changes on a wide scale since 1926 in
the Classification of Occupations and especially the introduction of Order XXVI bring-
ing together groups of unskilled workers not provided for elsewhere (formerly included
in various Orders) make it impracticable to give a general comparison between 1926 and
1951 figures.
It is, however, possible to give comparative figures for some occupa-
tions and the following are those which show the greatest variation in numbers and
which are broadly similar to the 1926 classification.
Occupation
Domestic Servants (indoor)
V/eavers (including carpet)
Laundry Workers
Makers of Tobacco, etc.
Nurses (other than Nursery)
Proportion per
1926
92
82

16

1, 000 of occupied females
1951
65
50
15
21
25
As regards Nurses, it should be noted that in 1926 certain occupations, e.g.,
Health and Tuberculosis Visitors were excluded from this classification.
It is not
possible to ascertain the numbers concerned, but it i's considered that the effect on
the figures quoted is negligible.
Table 20 shows the occupational distribution according to Marital Condition and
age.
Of the occupied males, 37.1 per cent are single, 59.8 per cent are married, and
3.1 per cent are widowed.

    The corresponding percentages for 1926 are 42.6, 52.7 and
4. 7 respectively.
Of the occupied females 69.4 per cent are single, 25.2 per cent are»married and
5.4 per cent are widowed.

    The figures for 1926 are 73.4, 19.5 and 7.1 respectively.
Of the total females employed as Textile Workers (Order VII) 38 per cent are married.
The percentage in the case of Makers of Textile Goods (IX) is 25 and for those engaged
in personal service 33.
Industries:- The industrial classification has not undergone widespread changes
since 1926 as was the case in the classification of occupations.
The 1926 and 1951
figures, however, are not comparable as the 1926 figures for the occupied population

    include persons out of work (i.e. 26,254 males and 10,291 females) whereas the 1951
figures exclude persons out of work, in accordance with the practice throughout the
United Kingdom for this Census.
Table XVIII shows the distribution for 1951 according to certain main industrial
groupings.

## Page 238

The Manufacturing Industries (used in a wide sense) provide employment for 56 per
cent of the occupied population (exclusive of persons temporarily out of work).
Dis-
tributive Trades gind Finance (including Insurance and Banking) provide 15 per cent and
Transport and Communication 8 per cent.
The 3926 figures v/hich include persons
temporarily out of work were - Manufacturing Industries 58 per cent, Distributive
Trades and Finance 16 per cent and Transport and Comm.unication 5 per cent.
The two main industries are Engineering (including Shipbuilding) and the Manu-
facture of Textiles.
The Table also shows that the Males putnumber the Females in all the groups except
the Professions and Miscellaneous Services.
The former group includes Teachers and
Nurses and the latter persons employed in Private Domestic Service, Restaurants,
Entertainment, etc.
Table 21 gives the industrial distribution of the occupied population in greater
detail, separate figure^ being given for each Order and suitable groupings thereunder.

County Borough
The figures for 1821 and 1831 exclude members of
2. The figures given for 1821 relate to the "Town" of Belfast; those for 1831 to the "Town and
Suburbs"; and those for subsequent years to the area of the Borough or County Borough as
constituted at the date of each Census.
The boundary of the Borough of Belfast, defined In
the Municipal Corporations (i.) Act, 1840, was extended by the Belfast Borough Extension Act,
,1853, and by the Belfast Corporation Act, 1896. By virtue of the Local Government (Ireland)
Act, 1898, the Borough became a County Borough.
3. It will be noted that since 1911 the censuses were taken at Irregular Intervals.

Note:- The Belfast Constituencies for united Kingdom elections are constituted as follows:-
East Belfast:
Dock, Pottinger and Victoria Wards.
North Belfast: Clifton, Duncalrn and Shanklll Wards.
South Belfast: Cromac, Ormeau and Windsor wards.
West Belfast:
Court, Falls, St. Anne's, St. George's, Smlthfleld and Woodvale Wards.

## Page 286

PREFACE
Three censuses of population have been taken since the Government of Northern
Irel&nd was established.
The first enumeration took place in 1926 and incorporated
questions relating to occupation and industry, orphanhood and infirmities.
The
second enumeration made in 1937 was of more limited scope and was intended to bridge
the gap between the census of 1926 and the census which it was proposed to take in
1941, but which had to be abandoned owing to the outbreak of war.
The census taken
as at mid-night of 8th-9th April, 1951, forms the basis of this report and like that
in 1926 questions were asked as to the occupations and industries of the population.
The length of time required to process the data collected at an enumeration before
it can be presented in the ultimate reports is necessarily considerable.
In order to
meet immediate requirements, however, two Preliminary Reports on the 1951 census were
published.
The first of these gave the population figures by administrative areas
and towns and villages, and by Counties and County Boroughs according to religious pro-
fession.
The Second Report, which was restricted to Counties and County Boroughs,
gave the population by age groups.
The present Volume is the third of seven county volumes (the Belfast County
Borough and County of Antrim volumes having been published already) and has been pre-
pared (as were also the Preliminary Reports) in accordance with the provisions of the
Census Act (Northern Ireland) 1951.
The statistics now published supersede those
shown in the Preliminary Reports and may be accepted as final.
This volume is consistent in design and scope with the previous volumes and the
remaining County volumes which will be published at intervals as completed will be
prepared in like manner.
A General Report for the whole of Northern Ireland will follow the county volumes.
This will bring together, and in some respects shovj in greater detail, the information
contained in the county volumes.
Additional features will also be introduced particu-
larly in regard to the distribution of the people industrially by age and marital
condition.
Reference has been made in the Preliminary Report and in the reports for Belfast
County Borough and County Antrim to the development which has taken place in the area
surrounding Belfast and the Preliminary Report gave the 1937 and 1951 populations of
the approximate area.
The matter has now been investigated in more detail than was
possible at the Preliminary Report stage and it is intended to include in a later
publication figures relating to population and housing in regard to what may be termed
the "Brick and Mortar" area of Belfast and its Environs.
Following the 1926 census a topographical index was published dealing with each
type of area for which statistics were supplied in the county series.
A supplement
to this indBx was published after the 1937 census and a further supplement in respect
of the 1951 census will be prepared in due course.
Census publications are obtainable either directly from H.M. Stationery Office,
Chichester House, Chichester Street, Belfast, or from any book-seller.
L. C. MULLIGAN,
REGISTRAR-GENERAL
General Register Office,
Fermanagh House,
Belfast.
March, 1954o
(14555)
