## Page 419

PREFACE
The First Preliminary Report of the Census of the population
taken on the 8th April, 1951 > was published in October 195*1 *
Consideration was given, to the question of publishing for
Northern Ireland a report containing analyses of the various
census characteristics of a representative sample of a percentage
of the population on the lines adopted in 1951 for the first
time by the Registrars-General of England and Wales and of
Scotland, but, when regard was had to the size and distribution
of the population in Northern Ireland, it was decided that the
work involved in the preparation of the material would not
warrant the adoption of that course.
It was apparent from several sources that there is an urgent
need, mainly in connection with medical and sociological
investigations, for reliable and up-to-date figures of ages of
the population and the decision was taken to issue a Second
Preliminary Report giving this information for Counties and
County Boroughs*
It was realised that it would be desirable to
give similar information for all Administrative Areas, but to
have done so would have unduly delayed the issue of the Report.
Despite efforts to obtain accurate information regarding age,
there is evidence that in certain sections of the age-field
approximate ages were stated; this is revealed by an unnatural
concentration at certain ages, more particularly those ending
with digit 0.
No adjustment in this respect has been made in the
published figures.
It was pointed out in the First Preliminary Report that the
information (except that relating to religion) contained in that
Report was derived from the Enumeration Books completed by the
Census Enumerators and that therefore the figures should be
regarded as provisional.
Several errors of a minor nature
were discovered in the comparison of the Enumeration Books with
the Household Returns, the net effect being to show an increase
of 224 in the population.
A complete verification of the
information published in this Report has not been possible in
the time available, but it is unlikely that the figures will
differ materially from the precise results to be incorporated in
the later Reports.
L.C. MULLIGAN
Registrar-General.
General Register Office,
Fermanagh House,
Ormeau Avenue,
BES^AST.
September, 1952*


---

## Page 420

*(blank or image-only page)*

---

## Page 421

STATISTICAL NOTES
During the half-century ended 1951 a steady increase in the
population of Northern Ireland has taken place.
In the first half
of this period the males increased by 18,135 and the females by
1,476 whereas during the second half the males increase by 59,733
and the females by 54,639*
The preponderance of females over
males at the beginning of the century, therefore, has been
substantially reduced; the proportion at the 1951 Census was

    1,053 females to every 1,000 males, compared with 1,097 females
to every 1,000 males in 1901.
From Table 2 which shows the percentage distribution of the
population in age-groups since 1841 it will.be observed that
Northern Ireland, in common with the rest of the United Kingdom,
contains an increasing proportion of older people.
In 1841 the
percentage aged 65 and over was 4*3 of the total pupulation; the
1951 figure is 9»9»
It is interesting to note the regularity of
decline in the number of children under 15 years of age between
1841 and 1937, and although this trend has now been arrested, the

    improvement between 1937 and 1951 is slight; viz., from 27*1 per
cent, to 27.6 per cent.
Whilst the 15-44 years age-group
increased between 1926 and 1937 the position was reversed during
the subsequent intercensal period for which a decrease is recorded
for both males and females. For the first*time, the number of
males in this group exceeded the number of females.
The following chart shows the distribution of the population

    in the four main age-groups for 1901, 1937 and 1951«

    Comparative figures for the Censuses of 1926, 1937 and 1951
are given for individual years in Table 3*
As in the 1926 and
1937 Censuses the 195*1 figures show that boys outnumber girls at
each year under 16 years of age.
Unlike the two earlier Censuses,
however, in 1951? boys continue to outnumber girls at each year up
to 19 years of age*
It will be observed that the number of persons aged 100 and

    over decreased from 30 in 1926 to 20 in 1937 and to 16 in 1951*
while the number of persons aged 80 and upwards, which fell from

    13,563 in 1926 to 13,361 in 1937, has risen to 19,846.
The number
of persons aged 80 and upwards at the beginning of the century v^as

    12,613 of whom 76 were recorded as 100 or over.
It will be seen from Table 4 that the increases in the numbers
of males and females since 1937 are approximately the same.
As
already mentioned, the superiority in numbers of females in 1951
does not take place until the age-group 20-24 years is reached.
With the exception of this age group which shows a slight increase
on the 1937 figures of the proportion of females there is a
decrease in the proportion of females until the age of 50 years is
reached; from that point until age 74 years the 1951 proportions
are higher than in 1937*

    For age-groups 75 to 79 to 85-89 the
proportions decrease, but from age 90 years onwards the
proportions again increase.
The most striking feature about Table 6 is that, despits a
gain of 5,594 in the total population of Belfast County Borough,
there has been a decrease of 23,410 in the number of persons aged

    from 10 to 39 years since 1937»
The reports yet to be published will provide a more suitable
opportunity to draw general conclusions from the data obtained,
about ages, particularly as the information can then be
related to occupations and industries, so far as it applies,
but it is felt that the tables in this Report will supply the
immediate demand for reliable information affecting many aspects
of the life of the community.
ii.
